Candidate algorithms: - Logistic Regression (baseline, interpretable) - Random Forest (handles non-linearity) - XGBoost (high performance) **Chosen:** Logistic Regression as baseline + XGBoost for production (compared via AUC-ROC). **Evaluation Metrics Selected:** Precision, Recall, F1-Score, AUC-ROC. **Justification for Logistic Regression Baseline:** $$P(\text{Churn} = 1 \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \dots + \beta_n x_n)}}$$ #### **Step 4: Model Building (Phase 4)** **Train/Test Split:** 80% training (40,000 records), 20% test (10,000 records), stratified by churn label. **5-Fold Cross-Validation on Training Set:** $$\text{CV Score} = \frac{1}{5}\sum_{k=1}^{5} F_1^{(k)}$$ Suppose the 5-fold F1-scores are: 0.81, 0.83, 0.79, 0.82, 0.80 $$\text{CV Score} = \frac{0.81 + 0.83 + 0.79 + 0.82 + 0.80}{5} = \frac{4.05}{5} = 0.81$$ **Confusion Matrix on Test Set (Hypothetical):** | | Predicted: No Churn | Predicted: Churn | |---|---|---| | **Actual: No Churn** | 7,200 (TN) | 400 (FP) | | **Actual: Churn** | 300 (FN) | 2,100 (TP) | **Metric Calculations:** $$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{2100}{2100 + 400} = \frac{2100}{2500} = 0.84$$ $$\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{2100}{2100 + 300} = \frac{2100}{2400} = 0.875$$ $$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.84 \times 0.875}{0.84 + 0.875} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.735}{1.715} = 2 \cdot 0.4286 = 0.857$$ #### **Step 5: Communicate Results (Phase 5)** **Dashboard Built:** Tableau dashboard showing: - Top-10 churn-risk features (Recency, Unresolved Tickets, Tenure) - Cohort-wise churn heatmap by city - Predicted churn vs. actual churn (lift chart) **Key Insight Communicated to SpiceKart Board:** > "Customers inactive for >60 days with ≥2 unresolved tickets have a **87.5% recall** in our model. Targeting this segment with a ₹200 discount voucher is projected to retain **~1,800 customers**, translating to a projected revenue saving of **₹1.2 Crores per quarter**." #### **Step 6: Operationalize (Phase 6)** **Deployment:** Model deployed as a **REST API** (Python Flask) on AWS EC2, scoring 50,000 customers every night via a **Spark batch job**. **Monitoring Metrics:** - Data drift score: $D_{KL} = \sum p_i(x) \log\left(\frac{p_i(x)}{q_i(x)}\right)$ (KL Divergence between training and live data distribution) - Alert triggered if $D_{KL} > 0.1$ → retraining pipeline activated. **A/B Test:** New XGBoost model vs. incumbent Logistic Regression on 10% traffic — XGBoost lifted F1 by **6.2%**, so fully rolled out. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: APM Phase vs. Key Deliverable vs. Tooling | Phase | Key Deliverable | Common Tools | Time Allocation (Typical) | |---|---|---|---| | 1. Discovery | Business objective doc, hypothesis statement | Stakeholder workshops, Jupyter Notebooks | 10–15% | | 2. Data Preparation | Clean, model-ready dataset (Parquet/CSV) | Pandas, PySpark, Talend, Informatica | 60–80% | | 3. Model Planning | Algorithm selection, evaluation metric choice | Scikit-learn docs, ML design docs | 5–10% | | 4. Model Building | Trained model artifact (`.pkl`, `.onnx`) | Scikit-learn, XGBoost, TensorFlow, MLflow | 5–10% | | 5. Communicate Results | Executive dashboard, narrative report | Tableau, Power BI, Matplotlib, Seaborn | 5% | | 6. Operationalize | Production API, monitoring dashboard | Docker, Kubernetes, Airflow, MLflow | 10–15% | ## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The Six-Phase Analytics Process Model (APM) — Master Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD A["PHASE 1: DISCOVERY
Business + Data Understanding"] --> B["PHASE 2: DATA PREPARATION
Cleansing, Transformation, Feature Engineering"] B --> C["PHASE 3: MODEL PLANNING
Algorithm + Metric Selection"] C --> D["PHASE 4: MODEL BUILDING
Training + Validation"] D --> E["PHASE 5: COMMUNICATE RESULTS
Dashboards + Storytelling"] E --> F["PHASE 6: OPERATIONALIZE
Deployment + Monitoring"] F --> G["ITERATION LOOP
New Insights or Drift Detected"] G --> A G --> B G --> C style A fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#0a3d62,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#a04000,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#0a5a0a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#d62728,stroke:#800000,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#9467bd,stroke:#4b2e6b,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#8c564b,stroke:#4a2c2a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px style G fill:#7f7f7f,stroke:#3d3d3d,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.2 Big Data Analytics Architecture — How APM Maps to Distributed Systems ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SRC["DATA SOURCES"] S1["Transactional DB"] S2["IoT Sensors"] S3["Social Media APIs"] S4["Log Files"] end subgraph P1["PHASE 1: DISCOVERY"] P1A["Business Objective"] P1B["Data Profiling"] end subgraph P2["PHASE 2: DATA PREPARATION"] P2A["HDFS Ingestion"] P2B["Spark Cleansing"] P2C["Feature Engineering"] end subgraph P3P4["PHASE 3 + 4: MODEL"] P3A["Algorithm Selection"] P3B["MLlib Training"] P3C["Cross Validation"] end subgraph P5["PHASE 5: COMMUNICATE"] P5A["Tableau Dashboard"] P5B["Executive Report"] end subgraph P6["PHASE 6: OPERATIONALIZE"] P6A["REST API Endpoint"] P6B["Drift Monitoring"] P6C["Airflow Retraining"] end S1 --> P1A S2 --> P1A S3 --> P1A S4 --> P1A P1A --> P2A P1B --> P2A P2A --> P2B P2B --> P2C P2C --> P3A P3A --> P3B P3B --> P3C P3C --> P5A P3C --> P5B P5A --> P6A P5B --> P6A P6A --> P6B P6B --> P6C P6C -.-> P2B style SRC fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#cc6600 style P1 fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0 style P2 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100 style P3P4 fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20 style P5 fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c style P6 fill:#fbe9e7,stroke:#bf360c ``` ### 4.3 Comparative Architecture: Data Analytics vs. Big Data Analytics Stack ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph DA["DATA ANALYTICS STACK (Single Machine)"] DA1["Excel / SQL Server"] DA2["Pandas / R"] DA3["Tableau / Power BI"] DA4["Report Output"] DA1 --> DA2 --> DA3 --> DA4 end subgraph BDA["BIG DATA ANALYTICS STACK (Cluster)"] BDA1["Kafka / Flume Ingestion"] BDA2["HDFS / S3 Storage"] BDA3["Spark / MapReduce Processing"] BDA4["Hive / HBase Query"] BDA5["Spark MLlib / Mahout"] BDA6["Tableau / Superset / Zeppelin"] BDA1 --> BDA2 --> BDA3 BDA3 --> BDA4 BDA3 --> BDA5 BDA4 --> BDA6 BDA5 --> BDA6 end style DA fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b style BDA fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f ``` ### 4.4 The Iteration Loop — Why APM is Cyclic, Not Linear ```mermaid flowchart TD Q["Insight Generated in Phase 5/6"] --> R{"New Business
Question?"} R -- YES --> D["Return to PHASE 1"] R -- NO --> S{"Model
Degradation?"} S -- YES --> DP["Return to PHASE 2"] S -- NO --> T{"Better Algorithm
Available?"} T -- YES --> MP["Return to PHASE 3"] T -- NO --> U["Continue Monitoring"] style Q fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100 style R fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17 style S fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17 style T fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17 style D fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1 style DP fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1 style MP fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1 style U fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation Hint:** The four Mermaid diagrams above collectively cover: (1) the master APM flow, (2) Big Data architecture mapping, (3) traditional vs. distributed stack comparison, and (4) the iteration principle. In KTU 14-mark questions, drawing a clean, well-labeled version of diagram (1) alone can fetch **3–4 marks** out of 14. ## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)** --- #### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]** *Define Big Data Analytics. List and briefly explain the five V's of Big Data.* **Model Answer (Board-Exam Standard):** **Definition:** Big Data Analytics is the process of examining large, complex, and diverse datasets — too massive for traditional data-processing tools — to uncover hidden patterns, correlations, market trends, customer preferences, and other useful business information. It uses distributed computing frameworks like Hadoop and Spark. **The 5 V's of Big Data:** 1. **Volume:** Refers to the sheer size of data. Big Data is typically in the range of **terabytes (TB)**, **petabytes (PB)**, or **exabytes (EB)**. *Example:* Facebook generates **4 petabytes** of data per day. 2. **Velocity:** Refers to the speed at which data is generated, collected, and processed. *Example:* Stock market data, IoT sensor readings, and social media feeds arrive in **real-time or near-real-time**. 3. **Variety:** Refers to the different types and formats of data. *Example:* **Structured** (SQL tables), **Semi-structured** (JSON, XML, NoSQL), and **Unstructured** (images, videos, emails, social media posts). 4. **Veracity:** Refers to the trustworthiness, quality, and accuracy of data. It addresses noise, biases, and inconsistencies in the data. 5. **Value:** Refers to the actual business worth extracted from Big Data. Value is the ultimate goal — turning raw data into actionable insight that drives decisions and revenue. > ⚠️ **Board Tip:** Examiners expect **bolded keywords** and **at least one example** per V. Missing examples cost 0.5 marks each. --- #### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]** *Differentiate between Data Analytics and Big Data Analytics with suitable examples.* **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Data Analytics | Big Data Analytics | |---|---|---| | **Data Volume** | Gigabytes to Terabytes | Terabytes to Exabytes | | **Data Variety** | Mostly **structured** (rows/columns) | Structured + Semi-structured + **Unstructured** (text, image, video) | | **Processing** | **Single machine** (laptop, workstation) | **Distributed cluster** (Hadoop, Spark) | | **Tools** | Excel, SQL, Tableau, R | Hadoop, Spark, Hive, Kafka, NoSQL | | **Speed** | Batch, periodic (daily/weekly reports) | **Real-time** or near-real-time | | **Example** | Monthly sales report of a regional store from SQL DB | Real-time fraud detection on credit card transactions across millions of users | | **Cost** | Low (single-machine license) | High (cluster infrastructure, cloud billing) | > ⚠️ **Board Tip:** Drawing the comparison table neatly fetches **full 3 marks**. Avoid one-line definitions — tabular comparison is the KTU valuation gold standard. --- ### **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)** --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Aligned to 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Describe the **Analytics Process Model (APM)** in detail. Explain all six phases with their objectives, key activities, and deliverables. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A retail company has collected **2 TB of customer transaction data** over 5 years from **15 regional stores**, including point-of-sale records, loyalty card swipes, and customer feedback forms. The company wants to build a **customer segmentation model**. Apply the **first two phases of the APM** (Discovery and Data Preparation) to this scenario. List the specific activities you would perform in each phase, identify at least **three data quality issues** you expect to encounter, and propose **two transformation techniques** with their mathematical formulations. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Answer for Question A:** **(a) The Analytics Process Model — Detailed Explanation (7 Marks):** The **Analytics Process Model (APM)** is a structured, iterative framework that prescribes the six ordered phases a data analyst must execute to convert raw data into business value. The six phases are: **1. Discovery (Phase 1) — 1 Mark** - **Objective:** Understand the business problem, identify available data, and frame the analytical question. - **Activities:** Stakeholder meetings, hypothesis formation, data source identification, initial data exploration (shape, schema, summary statistics). - **Deliverable:** A *Business Objective Document* and a *Hypothesis Statement*. **2. Data Preparation (Phase 2) — 1.5 Marks** - **Objective:** Clean, integrate, and transform raw data into a model-ready dataset. - **Activities:** Handling missing values, removing duplicates, outlier detection, data integration from heterogeneous sources, normalization, encoding categorical variables, feature engineering. - **Deliverable:** A clean, model-ready dataset (typically in Parquet/CSV format). - **Time consumption:** **60–80% of the project** (this is the most labor-intensive phase). **3. Model Planning (Phase 3) — 1 Mark** - **Objective:** Choose the appropriate algorithm, technique, and evaluation metric. - **Activities:** Algorithm selection (regression, classification, clustering), choosing metrics (RMSE, F1, AUC-ROC), designing train/test split and cross-validation strategy. - **Deliverable:** *Model Design Document*. **4. Model Building (Phase 4) — 1 Mark** - **Objective:** Train the model on prepared data, tune hyperparameters, and validate. - **Activities:** Model training, hyperparameter tuning (Grid Search, Random Search), k-Fold cross-validation, comparison of candidate models. - **Deliverable:** A trained model artifact (e.g., `.pkl`, `.onnx` file). **5. Communicate Results (Phase 5) — 1 Mark** - **Objective:** Translate technical findings into business insights for stakeholders. - **Activities:** Building dashboards (Tableau/Power BI), creating visualizations (ROC curves, lift charts), writing executive reports, storytelling. - **Deliverable:** *Executive Dashboard* and *Narrative Report*. **6. Operationalize (Phase 6) — 1 Mark** - **Objective:** Deploy the model into production and monitor its ongoing performance. - **Activities:** Deployment as REST API or batch job, A/B testing, data drift monitoring, scheduled retraining, governance and compliance. - **Deliverable:** *Production API* + *Monitoring Dashboard*. **Key Property:** The APM is **iterative and cyclic** — after deployment, new business questions or detected model degradation trigger a return to earlier phases. > **Mnemonic to remember:** **"D²-P²-M-C-O"** → **D**iscovery → **D**ata Prep → **P**lanning → **P**rototype/Build → **C**ommunicate → **O**perate. --- **(b) Applying APM Phases 1 & 2 to the Retail Segmentation Scenario (7 Marks):** **Phase 1: Discovery (3.5 Marks)** **Business Objective:** Segment customers into distinct groups (e.g., high-value, occasional, churn-risk) to enable targeted marketing campaigns. **Data Sources Identified:** - Point-of-Sale (POS) transactions from 15 stores — structured (CSV, SQL) - Loyalty card swipes — semi-structured (JSON with nested fields) - Customer feedback forms — unstructured (free-text comments) - Total volume: **2 TB** distributed across **5 years × 15 stores = 75 store-years** **Hypotheses Formulated:** - *H1:* Customers who shop in >3 different stores are higher spenders. - *H2:* Customers with >6 months of inactivity are likely to churn. - *H3:* Customers buying premium brands form a high-value cluster. **Initial EDA (Exploratory Data Analysis):** $$\bar{x}_{\text{transactions per customer}} = \frac{\text{Total transactions}}{\text{Total unique customers}}$$ $$\text{Median spend} = x_{\left(\frac{n+1}{2}\right)} \text{ (if } n \text{ odd)}$$ **Three Data Quality Issues Expected:** 1. **Missing Values:** Loyalty card swipes may be missing for cash-only customers → **Imputation strategies** (mean, median, KNN imputation). 2. **Inconsistent Formats:** Dates stored in different formats (`DD/MM/YYYY` vs. `MM-DD-YY`) across stores → **Schema standardization**. 3. **Duplicate Records:** Same loyalty card swiped twice at POS due to network glitches → **Deduplication using transaction ID + timestamp**. **Phase 2: Data Preparation (3.5 Marks)** **Two Transformation Techniques (with mathematical formulations):** **1. Min-Max Normalization (scaling `purchase_amount` to [0, 1]):** $$x_i^{\text{norm}} = \frac{x_i - \min(x)}{\max(x) - \min(x)}$$ For example, if `purchase_amount` ranges from ₹50 to ₹15,000, a transaction of ₹4,500 becomes: $$x_{\text{norm}} = \frac{4500 - 50}{15000 - 50} = \frac{4450}{14950} \approx 0.2977$$ This ensures all features contribute equally to distance-based clustering (like K-Means). **2. Z-Score Standardization (scaling `age` to mean 0, std 1):** $$z_i = \frac{x_i - \bar{x}}{s}$$ If mean age is **38 years** with std dev **12 years**, a customer aged 50 has: $$z = \frac{50 - 38}{12} = 1.0$$ **Outlier Detection using Z-Score:** Any $\vert z_i \vert > 3$ (e.g., age > 74 or age < 2) flagged as outlier. **Additional Activity — Encoding Categorical Variables (One-Hot Encoding):** For `store_location` (15 stores), create 15 binary columns: `is_store_1, is_store_2, ..., is_store_15`. **Feature Engineering — RFM Features:** - **R**ecency = `days_since_last_purchase` - **F**requency = `count_of_transactions` - **M**onetary = `total_spend` These three features become the primary input to the clustering algorithm in Phase 4. > **Final Output of Phase 2:** A clean, model-ready dataset of the form `(customer_id, recency, frequency, monetary, age_norm, is_store_1, ..., is_store_15)` ready for K-Means clustering. --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Aligned to 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Compare and contrast the **traditional Data Analytics pipeline** with the **Big Data Analytics pipeline**. Use a labeled diagram in your explanation. Explain how the Analytics Process Model maps onto a **Hadoop/Spark cluster** architecture. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A telecommunications company collects **call detail records (CDRs)** from **50 million subscribers** at a rate of **100,000 records/second**. The data is stored across **HDFS in JSON format**. Design a complete **Big Data Analytics Process Model** for detecting **fraudulent call patterns** in real-time. Specify the **tools, techniques, and algorithms** for each phase, and explain how the **iteration loop** would work in this streaming scenario. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Answer for Question B:** **(a) Traditional vs. Big Data Analytics Pipeline (7 Marks):** **Comparison Table (3 Marks):** | Aspect | Traditional Data Analytics | Big Data Analytics | |---|---|---| | **Data Scale** | GB–TB | TB–PB–EB | | **Data Type** | Structured (relational tables) | Structured + Semi-structured + Unstructured | | **Storage** | Single RDBMS (MySQL, Oracle) | Distributed (HDFS, S3, GCS) | | **Processing Engine** | Single-node (CPU-bound) | Distributed (MapReduce, Spark) | | **Latency** | Batch, hours to days | Real-time (milliseconds to seconds) | | **Cost** | Low (commodity server) | High (cluster of commodity nodes) | | **Failure Tolerance** | Single point of failure | Built-in replication, fault-tolerant | | **Use Case Example** | Monthly MIS report | Real-time fraud detection, recommendation engine | **Pipeline Diagram (2 Marks):** ``` Traditional: Source -> ETL -> RDBMS -> SQL/BI Tool -> Report Big Data: Source -> Kafka -> HDFS -> Spark -> MLlib -> Dashboard ``` **Mapping APM onto Hadoop/Spark (2 Marks):** - **Phase 1 (Discovery):** Use **Apache Ambari** for cluster visualization, **Hive metastore** for schema discovery, **Jupyter + PySpark** for EDA. - **Phase 2 (Data Preparation):** **Apache Spark** (PySpark DataFrame API) for cleansing, **Apache Pig** for ETL scripts, **Apache Sqoop** for RDBMS-to-HDFS import. - **Phase 3 (Model Planning):** Algorithm selection documented in ML design doc; uses **Spark MLlib** for distributed model training. - **Phase 4 (Model Building):** **Spark MLlib's** `LogisticRegression`, `RandomForestClassifier`, or **XGBoost-on-Spark** for distributed training. **MLflow** for experiment tracking. - **Phase 5 (Communicate Results):** **Apache Superset / Zeppelin / Tableau** dashboards backed by **Hive** or **Impala** queries. - **Phase 6 (Operationalize):** Model served via **Kafka + Spark Streaming** for real-time scoring, monitored via **Prometheus + Grafana**. --- **(b) Big Data APM for Real-Time CDR Fraud Detection (7 Marks):** **Scenario Recap:** 50 million subscribers, 100,000 records/sec streaming into HDFS as JSON. **Designed Analytics Process Model:** **Phase 1: Discovery (1 Mark)** - **Business Goal:** Detect fraudulent calls (e.g., international premium-rate number scams, SIM-box fraud) within 5 seconds of occurrence. - **Data Sources:** CDR streams from telecom switches (JSON), subscriber profile DB (HBase), known-fraud-number blacklist (Redis cache). - **Hypothesis:** Calls >3 SDs above the user's mean call duration OR to blacklisted numbers AND originating from a new IMEI indicate fraud. **Phase 2: Data Preparation (1.5 Marks)** - **Ingestion:** **Apache Kafka** topic `cdr-ingress` receives 100K records/sec; **Apache Flume** or **Kafka Connect** sinks to HDFS. - **Parsing:** **Spark Structured Streaming** parses JSON in micro-batches (1-second trigger). - **Cleaning:** Discard malformed JSON, deduplicate by `(subscriber_id, call_start_time)`, filter out test numbers. - **Feature Engineering:** Per-subscriber rolling features: `rolling_mean_call_duration_1h`, `rolling_unique_destinations_24h`, `imei_change_count_7d`. **Phase 3: Model Planning (1 Mark)** - **Problem Type:** Real-time binary classification (Fraud / Not-Fraud). - **Algorithms:** **Streaming Random Forest** (Spark MLlib), or **Online Gradient Boosting**. - **Metrics:** Precision@K (since fraud is rare, ~0.01% of calls), Recall, AUC-PR (Precision-Recall AUC). **Phase 4: Model Building (1 Mark)** - **Training:** Historical CDR data (1 year) used to train Random Forest in **Spark MLlib** with 200 trees, max depth 15. - **Cross-Validation:** Time-series split (NOT random k-fold, to avoid temporal leakage). - **Model Registration:** Stored in **MLflow Model Registry** with version control. **Phase 5: Communicate Results (1 Mark)** - **Real-time Dashboard:** **Grafana** panel showing fraud rate per minute, top 10 fraud patterns, geo-heatmap of fraud origins. - **Alert System:** **Apache Kafka** publishes fraud alerts to a `fraud-alerts` topic → consumed by the customer-care dashboard and SMS gateway. **Phase 6: Operationalize (1 Mark)** - **Deployment:** Model deployed as a **Spark Structured Streaming** job running on a **YARN/Kubernetes** cluster, scoring each CDR in <500 ms. - **Monitoring:** Drift detection via **KL divergence** between training and live feature distributions; alert if $D_{KL} > 0.1$. - **Retraining:** Weekly **Airflow DAG** retrains model on the last 30 days of labeled data. **Iteration Loop (0.5 Mark):** 1. **New Fraud Pattern Emerges** (e.g., SIM-box fraud using VoIP) → Return to **Phase 1** to update hypothesis. 2. **Model Accuracy Degrades** (recall drops below 0.85) → Return to **Phase 2** to engineer new features (e.g., VoIP-call detection). 3. **Better Algorithm Available** (e.g., transformer-based fraud detection) → Return to **Phase 3** to re-plan. The streaming nature of CDR data makes the APM loop **continuous** — every hour, the system scores, monitors, and decides whether to iterate. > ⚠️ **Board Tip for Part B (14-Mark Questions):** Always structure your answer with **numbered headings for each sub-part** (`(a)`, `(b)`) and **bullet points within each phase**. Examiners scan for these structural cues. A well-labeled diagram fetches **2–3 marks** even if the explanation is brief. --- > [!WARNING] > **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Confusing APM with CRISP-DM:** Students often write CRISP-DM phases (Business Understanding, Data Understanding, …) when the question asks for APM. CRISP-DM has 6 phases but they are named differently. **KTU expects APM terminology (Discovery → Data Preparation → Model Planning → Model Building → Communicate Results → Operationalize).** > 2. **Forgetting the "Iterative" Property:** Stating that APM is strictly linear costs **1 mark** in 7-mark questions. Always close with: *"The APM is iterative — after deployment, findings often trigger a return to earlier phases."* > 3. **Mixing up "Data Analytics" and "Big Data Analytics":** KTU distinguishes these clearly. Data Analytics = single-machine + structured; Big Data Analytics = cluster + multi-format. > 4. **Skipping examples in 3-mark questions:** 3-mark Part A questions expect **at least one real-world example** (e.g., "e.g., Facebook generates 4 PB/day"). Without examples, expect 0.5 mark deduction. > 5. **In 14-mark questions, omitting tools:** When describing a phase, always mention **at least 1 tool** (e.g., "Spark MLlib for Phase 4"). Tool-less answers are penalized. > 6. **Math errors in normalization:** A single wrong calculation in Min-Max or Z-score normalization costs **1 full mark** in 7-mark sub-questions. Show all intermediate steps. --- ### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember** 📋 > [!IMPORTANT] > **🚀 High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Must Memorize Before Exam)** #### **Core Definitions** - ☐ **Data Analytics** = systematic computational analysis of data → insight - ☐ **Big Data Analytics** = analytics on Volume/Velocity/Variety/Veracity/Value data using distributed systems - ☐ **Analytics Process Model (APM)** = 6-phase iterative lifecycle converting raw data → deployed insight #### **The 5 V's of Big Data** - ☐ **Volume** (size), **Velocity** (speed), **Variety** (types), **Veracity** (trust), **Value** (business worth) #### **The 6 APM Phases (Mnemonic: D²-P²-M-C-O)** - ☐ **Phase 1 — Discovery:** Business understanding, data exploration, hypothesis - ☐ **Phase 2 — Data Preparation:** Cleansing, integration, transformation (60–80% of project time) - ☐ **Phase 3 — Model Planning:** Algorithm + metric selection - ☐ **Phase 4 — Model Building:** Training, cross-validation, hyperparameter tuning - ☐ **Phase 5 — Communicate Results:** Dashboards, storytelling, executive reports - ☐ **Phase 6 — Operationalize:** Deployment, monitoring, retraining #### **Must-Know Formulas** - ☐ Mean: $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum x_i$ - ☐ Variance: $s^2 = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2$ - ☐ Min-Max Normalization: $x_{\text{norm}} = \frac{x - \min}{\max - \min}$ - ☐ Z-Score: $z = \frac{x - \bar{x}}{s}$ - ☐ Precision, Recall, F1, R², RMSE #### **Key Differences (Frequently Asked)** - ☐ Data Analytics vs. Big Data Analytics (Volume, Tools, Speed, Cost) - ☐ Structured vs. Semi-structured vs. Unstructured data - ☐ Batch processing vs. Real-time streaming - ☐ Model accuracy vs. Business value (Operationalize is about Value, not just accuracy) #### **Iteration Principle** - ☐ APM is **cyclic, not linear** — always mention this in long-answer questions - ☐ Iteration is triggered by: new business question, model degradation, data drift, new algorithms #### **High-Yield 3-Mark Triggers** - ☐ "Define Big Data Analytics" + 5 V's - ☐ "Differentiate Data Analytics and Big Data Analytics" - ☐ "What is the Analytics Process Model?" - ☐ "Why is Data Preparation the most time-consuming phase?" #### **High-Yield 14-Mark Triggers** - ☐ "Explain all six phases of APM with examples" - ☐ "Apply APM to [Healthcare/Finance/Retail] scenario" - ☐ "Design a Big Data pipeline for [fraud/IoT/recommendation]" - ☐ "Map APM onto Hadoop/Spark architecture"

topics.title: Analytical Model Requirements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT864 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Analytical Model Requirements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — ANALYTICAL MODEL REQUIREMENTS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (2024 Scheme Aligned)**
> **Analytical Model Requirements** are the set of functional, non-functional, statistical, computational, and domain-specific specifications that must be satisfied when designing, building, validating, and deploying analytical models over large-scale, heterogeneous, and high-velocity data ecosystems. These requirements define *what the model must do, how well it must do it, on what data, with what resources, and within what constraints.*

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, analytical model requirements sit at the intersection of **Data Engineering**, **Statistical Learning**, and **Distributed Computing**. They are not just "model accuracy" — they form a **holistic contract** between the data scientist, the business stakeholder, and the infrastructure team.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital Diagnostics Lab"

Imagine an analytical model is like a **pathology laboratory** in a hospital.

| Lab Element | Analytical Model Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Patient samples (blood, tissue) | Raw Big Data (logs, clicks, sensors) |
| Quality of the sample (contamination, age) | Data quality, veracity, freshness |
| Test equipment (machines, reagents) | Algorithms, frameworks (Hadoop, Spark, TensorFlow) |
| Skilled pathologist | Data scientist / ML engineer |
| Diagnostic accuracy | Model precision, recall, F1 |
| Report turnaround time | Latency (real-time vs batch) |
| Reproducibility of results | Model determinism & versioning |
| Regulatory compliance (NABL) | Privacy, governance (GDPR, HIPAA) |
| Cost per test | Compute & storage cost |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**
> Just as a pathologist cannot issue a report from a contaminated sample, an analytical model trained on poor-quality big data will produce **misleading insights at scale** — the *amplification effect* of big data. Requirements exist to *prevent this amplification of error*.

### 1.2 The Five Pillars of Analytical Model Requirements

The KTU 2024 framework organizes requirements into **five inter-locking pillars**:

1. **Data Requirements** — Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value (the 5Vs)
2. **Statistical & Mathematical Requirements** — Accuracy, generalization, convergence
3. **Computational & Infrastructure Requirements** — Scalability, fault-tolerance, latency
4. **Business & Domain Requirements** — ROI, interpretability, fairness
5. **Operational Requirements** — Deployment, monitoring, lifecycle management

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip (KTU Board Pattern):** A 14-mark question on this topic almost always asks for a *classification of requirements with examples*. Memorize the five pillars and one concrete example under each.

---

### 1.3 Why "Requirements" Matter in Big Data Context

In classical small-data analytics, a model failure is **local and bounded**. In big data analytics, a model failure can be:

- **Amplified** — errors replicated across billions of records
- **Cascading** — feeding downstream models and dashboards
- **Latent** — discovered only after production damage
- **Costly** — compute cost of retraining terabyte-scale pipelines

Hence the **shift from "model-centric" to "requirements-centric"** thinking in the KTU 2024 curriculum.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of Each Pillar

### Pillar 1 — Data Requirements

- **Volume Requirement:** Model must handle petabyte-scale training sets via distributed representations.
- **Velocity Requirement:** Streaming models (e.g., online learning, Kafka + Flink) must process events with sub-second latency.
- **Variety Requirement:** Schema-flexible ingestion (Parquet, Avro, JSON, images, text).
- **Veracity Requirement:** Noise, missingness, bias detection routines.
- **Value Requirement:** Feature engineering pipeline that maximizes information gain per byte.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stat:** Industry benchmarks (cited in KTU reference material) show that **data scientists spend ≈ 60–80% of their time on data preparation**, not modeling. Requirements engineering must therefore *front-load* data quality specifications.

### Pillar 2 — Statistical & Mathematical Requirements

- **Accuracy:** Closeness of predictions to ground truth.
- **Precision & Recall:** Class-imbalance aware metrics.
- **Generalization:** Low variance between training and test performance → low *overfitting gap*.
- **Convergence:** For iterative algorithms (gradient descent, EM, k-means), the loss function must reach a stable minimum within a bounded number of iterations.
- **Statistical Significance:** Hypothesis testing to ensure observed patterns are not random.
- **Calibration:** Predicted probabilities must reflect true frequencies (Brier score).

### Pillar 3 — Computational & Infrastructure Requirements

- **Scalability:** Horizontal (add nodes) and vertical (add cores/RAM).
- **Parallelism:** Data-parallel vs model-parallel.
- **Fault Tolerance:** Checkpointing, lineage (RDD in Spark).
- **Latency Budget:** Real-time (< 1s), near-real-time (< 1 min), batch (hours).
- **Cost Function:** Cost = f(data size, model complexity, iteration count, infra type).

### Pillar 4 — Business & Domain Requirements

- **Interpretability:** White-box vs black-box trade-off.
- **Actionability:** Output must drive a business decision.
- **Fairness & Bias:** Demographic parity, equalized odds.
- **ROI Computation:** Net benefit of model vs cost of building + operating it.

### Pillar 5 — Operational Requirements

- **Reproducibility:** Same data + code + hyper-parameters → same result.
- **Versioning:** Data version, model version, code version (DVC, MLflow).
- **Monitoring:** Data drift, concept drift, performance decay.
- **Retraining Policy:** Scheduled vs triggered.
- **Governance:** Audit trails, model cards, lineage.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **examination-critical**. Use the table for last-mile revision.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Unit / Type | Notes for KTU |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mean Squared Error | $MSE = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Continuous metric (↓ better) | Sensitive to outliers |
| 2 | Root Mean Squared Error | $RMSE = \sqrt{MSE}$ | Same unit as $y$ | Preferred for reporting |
| 3 | Mean Absolute Error | $MAE = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$ | Same unit as $y$ | Robust to outliers |
| 4 | R-Squared (Coefficient of Determination) | $R^2 = 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}}$ where $SS_{res}=\sum(y_i-\hat{y}_i)^2$, $SS_{tot}=\sum(y_i-\bar{y})^2$ | Dimensionless ∈ [−∞, 1] | Measures variance explained |
| 5 | Accuracy | $Accuracy = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Ratio ∈ [0, 1] | Misleading on imbalanced data |
| 6 | Precision | $Precision = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Ratio ∈ [0, 1] | "How many predicted positives are correct" |
| 7 | Recall (Sensitivity / TPR) | $Recall = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Ratio ∈ [0, 1] | "How many actual positives are caught" |
| 8 | F1-Score | $F1 = 2 \cdot \frac{Precision \cdot Recall}{Precision + Recall}$ | Ratio ∈ [0, 1] | Harmonic mean of P & R |
| 9 | Specificity (TNR) | $Specificity = \frac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | Ratio ∈ [0, 1] | Important in medical analytics |
| 10 | Brier Score (Calibration) | $BS = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(\hat{p}_i - o_i)^2$ where $o_i \in \{0,1\}$ | Continuous (↓ better) | Measures probability calibration |
| 11 | Confusion Rate | $CR = \frac{FP + FN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Ratio ∈ [0, 1] | Complement of accuracy |
| 12 | Generalization Gap | $\Delta_{gen} = \vert E_{train} - E_{test} \vert$ | Same unit as loss | If large → overfitting |
| 13 | Bias-Variance Decomposition | $E[(y-\hat{f})^2] = Bias^2 + Variance + \sigma^2_{irred}$ | Sum of squared errors | Foundation of model trade-off |
| 14 | Amdahl's Law (Speedup) | $S = \frac{1}{(1-p) + \frac{p}{N}}$ where $p$ = parallel fraction, $N$ = processors | Speedup factor | Critical for big data scalability |
| 15 | Gustafson's Law (Scaled Speedup) | $S' = N - (1-p)(N-1)$ | Speedup factor | Weak-scaling counterpart |
| 16 | Cost Function (Composite) | $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{n}\sum L(\hat{y}_i, y_i) + \lambda \Vert \theta \Vert^2$ | Scalar loss + reg. | Includes $L_2$ regularization |
| 17 | Information Gain (Entropy-based) | $IG(S,A) = H(S) - \sum_{v \in Values(A)}\frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}H(S_v)$ | Bits | Used in decision tree feature selection |
| 18 | KL Divergence (Drift Detection) | $D_{KL}(P\Vert Q) = \sum P(x)\log\frac{P(x)}{Q(x)}$ | Non-negative, asymmetric | Concept drift quantification |
| 19 | Throughput Requirement | $T = \frac{N_{records}}{t_{window}}$ | records/sec | Streaming SLA |
| 20 | Latency Requirement | $L = t_{event} - t_{prediction}$ | milliseconds | Real-time SLA |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of Each Requirement Category

| Requirement Type | Engineering Use Case |
|---|---|
| Data Veracity | Fraud detection in banking — noisy labels cause 20–30% false positives |
| Velocity | Click-stream ad-bidding (100 ms budget per impression) |
| Scalability | Genomics analytics on 100 TB+ sequencing data |
| Interpretability | Medical diagnosis (clinicians demand SHAP/LIME explanations) |
| Fairness | Loan-approval models (RBI / EU regulations) |
| Reproducibility | Pharmaceutical drug-discovery pipelines (FDA audit) |
| Cost | Edge-AI on IoT fleets with battery & bandwidth constraints |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked *"Why is X a requirement in big data?"*, always frame the answer as **Volume × Velocity × Veracity trade-off** — examiners reward this triad.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — R² from First Principles

The **coefficient of determination** is derived from the decomposition of total variance.

**Step 1: Define Total Sum of Squares (SStot).**
This measures the total variation in the observed target $y$ around its mean $\bar{y}$.

$$
SStot = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \bar{y})^2
$$

**Step 2: Define Residual Sum of Squares (SSres).**
This measures the unexplained variation — the error the model still makes.

$$
SSres = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2
$$

**Step 3: Define Regression Sum of Squares (SSreg).**
This measures the variation explained by the model.

$$
SSreg = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(\hat{y}_i - \bar{y})^2
$$

**Step 4: Establish the identity.**

$$
SStot = SSreg + SSres
$$

**Step 5: Define R² as the fraction of explained variation.**

$$
R^2 = \frac{SSreg}{SStot} = 1 - \frac{SSres}{SStot}
$$

**Step 6: Algebraic expansion of the alternative form.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R^2 &= 1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \bar{y})^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why this matters for analytical model requirements:** $R^2$ is a **mandatory reporting metric** in KTU valuation. A high $R^2$ on training data with a low $R^2$ on test data signals **overfitting**, which is a *violation* of the generalization requirement.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — F1-Score from Precision & Recall

The F1-score is the **harmonic mean** of precision and recall, derived as follows.

**Step 1: Recall the harmonic mean definition for two numbers $a$ and $b$.**

$$
HM(a,b) = \frac{2ab}{a + b}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute $a = Precision$ and $b = Recall$.**

$$
F1 = \frac{2 \cdot Precision \cdot Recall}{Precision + Recall}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute the full expressions.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Precision &= \frac{TP}{TP + FP} \\[4pt]
Recall &= \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Combine into a single ratio.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F1 &= \frac{2 \cdot \frac{TP}{TP+FP} \cdot \frac{TP}{TP+FN}}{\frac{TP}{TP+FP} + \frac{TP}{TP+FN}} \\[6pt]
   &= \frac{2 \cdot TP^2}{TP(TP+FN) + TP(TP+FP)} \cdot \frac{1}{1} \\[6pt]
   &= \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2TP + FP + FN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final simplified form (commonly used in KTU numericals):**

$$
F1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2TP + FP + FN}
$$

**Why this matters:** In imbalanced big-data classification (e.g., fraud ≈ 0.01% of transactions), accuracy can be 99.99% yet the model is *useless*. F1 corrects for this — a **mandatory requirement** for any production classifier.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Amdahl's Law (Scalability Requirement)

**Step 1: Let $T(1)$ = execution time on 1 processor.**

**Step 2: Partition the workload into a serial fraction $(1-p)$ and a parallel fraction $p$.**

**Step 3: On $N$ processors, parallel fraction runs in $\frac{p \cdot T(1)}{N}$.**

**Step 4: Total time on $N$ processors.**

$$
T(N) = (1-p)T(1) + \frac{p \cdot T(1)}{N}
$$

**Step 5: Speedup is the ratio.**

$$
S(N) = \frac{T(1)}{T(N)} = \frac{T(1)}{(1-p)T(1) + \frac{p T(1)}{N}} = \frac{1}{(1-p) + \frac{p}{N}}
$$

**Step 6: Limiting case as $N \to \infty$.**

$$
\lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) = \frac{1}{1-p}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Implication:** Even with **infinite** processors, speedup is bounded by $\frac{1}{1-p}$. This proves that a model with 5% serial overhead is capped at $20\times$ speedup — a **scalability requirement violation** for petascale analytics.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Requirement ↔ Metric ↔ Failure Mode

| Requirement | Quantitative Metric | Threshold (Typical) | Failure Mode if Violated |
|---|---|---|---|
| Accuracy | $F1$ / $RMSE$ | $F1 \geq 0.85$ | Misclassification cascade |
| Latency | $L$ (ms) | $L \leq 100$ ms (real-time) | SLA breach, revenue loss |
| Throughput | $T$ (rec/s) | $T \geq 10^5$ rec/s | Backlog, dropped events |
| Scalability | $S(N)$ (Amdahl) | $S(N) \geq 0.7 \cdot N$ | Cluster under-utilization |
| Generalization | $\Delta_{gen}$ | $\Delta_{gen} \leq 0.05$ | Overfitting |
| Calibration | Brier Score | $BS \leq 0.1$ | Mis-calibrated probabilities |
| Fairness | Demographic Parity Diff. | $\vert DP \vert \leq 0.05$ | Discriminatory outcomes |
| Reproducibility | Run-to-Run variance | $\sigma^2 \leq 10^{-4}$ | Audit failure |
| Data Quality | Missing-value rate | $\leq 2\%$ | Biased model |
| Cost | \$ / prediction | Domain-specific | Unsustainable deployment |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Framework of Analytical Model Requirements

```mermaid
graph TD
    ROOT[Analytical Model Requirements]
    P1[Pillar 1 Data Requirements]
    P2[Pillar 2 Statistical Requirements]
    P3[Pillar 3 Computational Requirements]
    P4[Pillar 4 Business Requirements]
    P5[Pillar 5 Operational Requirements]

    ROOT --> P1
    ROOT --> P2
    ROOT --> P3
    ROOT --> P4
    ROOT --> P5

    P1 --> N1A[Volume Handling]
    P1 --> N1B[Velocity Streaming]
    P1 --> N1C[Variety Schema]
    P1 --> N1D[Veracity Quality]
    P1 --> N1E[Value Information Gain]

    P2 --> N2A[Accuracy F1 RMSE]
    P2 --> N2B[Generalization Gap]
    P2 --> N2C[Convergence Stability]
    P2 --> N2D[Calibration Brier]
    P2 --> N2E[Significance p value]

    P3 --> N3A[Scalability Amdahl]
    P3 --> N3B[Latency SLA]
    P3 --> N3C[Throughput SLA]
    P3 --> N3D[Fault Tolerance]
    P3 --> N3E[Cost Efficiency]

    P4 --> N4A[Interpretability]
    P4 --> N4B[Actionability]
    P4 --> N4C[Fairness Bias]
    P4 --> N4D[ROI Justification]

    P5 --> N5A[Reproducibility]
    P5 --> N5B[Versioning]
    P5 --> N5C[Drift Monitoring]
    P5 --> N5D[Retraining Policy]
    P5 --> N5E[Governance Audit]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The five pillars operate as a *layered contract*. Pillar 1–2 are *data-side*, Pillars 3–4 are *system-side*, and Pillar 5 is the *lifecycle glue* that keeps the model valid post-deployment.

---

## 4.2 Requirement Validation Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Big Data Sources] --> B[Ingestion Layer]
    B --> C[Data Quality Gate]
    C -->|Pass| D[Feature Engineering]
    C -->|Fail| X[Quarantine and Alert]
    D --> E[Model Training Engine]
    E --> F[Statistical Validation]
    F -->|Pass| G[Computational Profiling]
    F -->|Fail| H[Re-tune and Re-train]
    G -->|Pass| I[Business Stakeholder Review]
    G -->|Fail| J[Cluster Right-sizing]
    I -->|Approved| K[Deployment to Production]
    I -->|Rejected| H
    K --> L[Continuous Monitoring]
    L -->|Drift Detected| H
    L -->|Stable| M[Sustain Operations]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Drawing Tip:** This block diagram is *exactly* the kind of figure that earns full marks in a 14-mark descriptive question. Examiners award 1–2 marks for clean, labelled flow diagrams.

---

## 4.3 Cross-Pillar Trade-off Matrix (Schematic)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[High Accuracy] ---|conflicts with| B[High Latency]
    C[High Interpretability] ---|conflicts with| D[High Complexity]
    E[High Scalability] ---|conflicts with| F[Low Cost]
    G[High Throughput] ---|conflicts with| H[Low Latency]
    I[High Fairness] ---|conflicts with| J[High Accuracy on Biased Data]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Reality:** No real-world big-data analytical model satisfies *all* requirements maximally. The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly tests whether students can **prioritize and justify trade-offs**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List any six analytical model requirements relevant to big data analytics and classify them under data, statistical, computational, business, or operational categories.**

**Model Answer (6 points × 0.5 = 3 marks):**

1. **Data Quality (Data):** Missing-value rate ≤ 2% is mandatory for reliable training.
2. **Scalability (Computational):** Amdahl's-law based speedup target S(N) ≥ 0.7·N.
3. **Latency (Computational):** Real-time SLA — prediction within 100 ms.
4. **Accuracy (Statistical):** F1-score ≥ 0.85 on imbalanced classes.
5. **Interpretability (Business):** SHAP / LIME explanations for stakeholder trust.
6. **Reproducibility (Operational):** Identical outputs across re-runs under fixed seeds.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall — Part A:** Students often *list* requirements without *categorizing* them. KTU's marking scheme deducts 1 mark if categorization is missing.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Differentiate between statistical significance requirement and business significance requirement in an analytical model.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Statistical Significance | Business Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Probability that observed effect is not due to chance ($p < 0.05$) | Practical impact of the model effect on business KPIs |
| Metric | $p$-value, confidence interval | ROI, conversion lift, cost saving |
| Threshold | Universal ($p \leq 0.05$) | Domain-specific (≥ 5% revenue lift, etc.) |
| Decided by | Data scientist | Business stakeholder |

A model can be **statistically significant** but **not business significant** (e.g., 0.1% CTR lift with high implementation cost).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall — Part A:** Writing the difference as *"one uses p-value, the other uses money"* loses 1 mark. Always include *who decides* and *threshold type*.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**"Analytical Model Requirements in big data analytics are multi-dimensional and often conflicting." Discuss with reference to the five pillars of requirements — Data, Statistical, Computational, Business, and Operational. For each pillar, list at least three concrete requirements with quantitative metrics.**

#### (a) Detailed Discussion of the Five Pillars — 7 Marks

**Pillar 1 — Data Requirements (1.4 marks)**
- **Volume:** Model must handle training sets ≥ 1 TB using distributed storage (HDFS, S3).
- **Velocity:** Streaming pipelines (Kafka + Flink) with end-to-end latency ≤ 1 s.
- **Veracity:** Noise filtering, missing-value imputation, schema validation.
- **Metric:** $Q = 1 - \frac{N_{missing}}{N_{total}}$, target $Q \geq 0.98$.

**Pillar 2 — Statistical Requirements (1.4 marks)**
- **Accuracy:** $F1 \geq 0.85$ on imbalanced classification.
- **Generalization:** $\Delta_{gen} = \vert E_{train} - E_{test} \vert \leq 0.05$.
- **Calibration:** Brier score $BS \leq 0.1$.
- **Convergence:** Loss plateau within $10^4$ iterations.

**Pillar 3 — Computational Requirements (1.4 marks)**
- **Scalability:** $S(N) = \frac{1}{(1-p) + \frac{p}{N}} \geq 0.7N$ per Amdahl.
- **Latency:** $L \leq 100$ ms (real-time), $L \leq 1$ min (near-real-time).
- **Fault Tolerance:** Recovery time objective (RTO) ≤ 5 min.
- **Cost:** $C = \alpha \cdot D + \beta \cdot M + \gamma \cdot T$, where $D$ = data size, $M$ = model size, $T$ = training time.

**Pillar 4 — Business Requirements (1.4 marks)**
- **Interpretability:** SHAP / LIME mandatory in regulated domains.
- **Fairness:** Demographic parity difference $\vert DP \vert \leq 0.05$.
- **ROI:** Net benefit $\geq 3\times$ total cost of ownership (TCO).

**Pillar 5 — Operational Requirements (1.4 marks)**
- **Reproducibility:** Run-to-run variance $\sigma^2 \leq 10^{-4}$.
- **Drift Monitoring:** KL-divergence $D_{KL}(P_{today} \Vert P_{baseline}) \leq 0.1$ triggers retraining.
- **Versioning:** Model + data + config versions stored in MLflow / DVC.
- **Governance:** Model card + audit log + signed approvals.

---

#### (b) Conflict Resolution & Trade-off Analysis — 7 Marks

Real-world analytical models rarely satisfy *all* pillars simultaneously. Common conflicts and resolutions:

| Conflict | Example | Resolution Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| Accuracy vs Latency | Deep neural net with 50 ms inference | Use model distillation to a smaller student model |
| Interpretability vs Complexity | XGBoost vs Linear Regression | Use SHAP on the XGBoost to maintain both |
| Scalability vs Cost | 100-node Spark cluster idle 80% | Spot instances + auto-scaling |
| Throughput vs Latency | Batch ML vs online learning | Hybrid: micro-batch (1 s windows) |
| Fairness vs Accuracy | Equalized odds may reduce AUC | Post-processing calibration (e.g., reject-option classification) |
| Reproducibility vs Velocity | Stream re-processing is expensive | Use deterministic seeds + idempotent sinks |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall — Part B (7-mark sub-part):** Students write *only the pillars* and forget the *trade-off resolution*. The 7-mark sub-part explicitly demands *conflict analysis*. Missing this = −3 to −4 marks.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**Explain the statistical and computational requirements for building an analytical model on big data. Derive the F1-score formula and apply Amdahl's Law to compute the speedup of a distributed training job where 8% of the workload is inherently serial, running on 64 nodes.**

#### (a) Statistical & Computational Requirements — 7 Marks

**Statistical Requirements (3.5 marks):**
1. **Accuracy / F1 / RMSE** must meet domain-acceptable thresholds.
2. **Generalization gap** $\Delta_{gen} \leq 0.05$ to avoid overfitting.
3. **Convergence** of iterative optimizers (gradient descent, k-means) within bounded iterations.
4. **Significance** via $p$-value $\leq 0.05$ or confidence interval non-overlap.
5. **Calibration** via Brier score for probabilistic outputs.
6. **Robustness** to noisy labels (e.g., $\epsilon$-contamination model).

**Computational Requirements (3.5 marks):**
1. **Horizontal Scalability** via Amdahl / Gustafson.
2. **Latency SLA** (real-time vs batch).
3. **Throughput SLA** (records/sec).
4. **Fault Tolerance** via checkpointing.
5. **Cost per prediction** ≤ business threshold.
6. **Resource elasticity** for cloud deployment.

#### (b) F1 Derivation + Amdahl Numerical — 7 Marks

**F1 Derivation (3.5 marks):**

Start from the harmonic mean of $P$ (precision) and $R$ (recall):

$$
F1 = \frac{2PR}{P + R}
$$

Substitute $P = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ and $R = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F1 &= \frac{2 \cdot \frac{TP}{TP+FP} \cdot \frac{TP}{TP+FN}}{\frac{TP}{TP+FP} + \frac{TP}{TP+FN}} \\[6pt]
   &= \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2TP + FP + FN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Amdahl's Law Numerical (3.5 marks):**

Given $p = 0.08$ (parallel fraction), $N = 64$ nodes.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(64) &= \frac{1}{(1 - 0.08) + \frac{0.08}{64}} \\[4pt]
      &= \frac{1}{0.92 + 0.00125} \\[4pt]
      &= \frac{1}{0.92125} \\[4pt]
      &\approx 1.0855
\end{aligned}
$$

**Limiting speedup as $N \to \infty$:**

$$
S_{\infty} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.08} = \frac{1}{0.92} \approx 1.0869
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark bonus):**
With 92% serial overhead, even a 64-node cluster yields only **8.55% speedup** — a clear **scalability requirement violation**. Recommendation: refactor the serial section (e.g., shared I/O, global lock) before adding more nodes.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall — Part B Numerical:** Students often *forget the serial fraction $(1-p)$* in Amdahl's formula. KTU key checks: if you write $\frac{1}{p/N}$, you lose 2 full marks.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- **Definition:** Analytical Model Requirements = the contract specifying *what* an analytical model must deliver, *on what data*, *under what constraints*.
- **Five Pillars:** Data, Statistical, Computational, Business, Operational.
- **5Vs of Big Data → Data Requirements:** Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value.
- **Statistical Metrics to Memorize:** MSE, RMSE, MAE, R², Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1, Brier Score.
- **Computational Laws to Memorize:** Amdahl's Law (fixed-size) and Gustafson's Law (scaled-size).
- **Generalization:** Always check the **train-test gap** $\Delta_{gen}$.
- **Trade-offs:** Accuracy vs Latency, Interpretability vs Complexity, Scalability vs Cost.
- **Operational:** Reproducibility + Versioning + Drift Monitoring + Governance.
- **Drift Detection:** KL-divergence $D_{KL}$ and Population Stability Index (PSI).
- **Formula for F1 in one line:** $F1 = \frac{2TP}{2TP + FP + FN}$.
- **Formula for R² in one line:** $R^2 = 1 - \frac{SSres}{SStot}$.
- **Amdahl one-liner:** $S = \frac{1}{(1-p) + \frac{p}{N}}$.
- **Implication of Amdahl:** $\lim_{N\to\infty} S = \frac{1}{1-p}$ — small serial fractions cap speedup dramatically.
- **KTU Mantra for this topic:** *"In big data, requirements engineering is the model."* — start with requirements before writing a single line of code.
- **Common Examiner Reward:** Clean block diagrams and quantitative trade-off tables → +2 marks.
- **Common Examiner Penalty:** Skipping the *operational pillar* → −1 mark in 14-mark answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Analytics Life Cycle Overview
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT864 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Data Analytics Life Cycle Overview

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Data Analytics Life Cycle (DALC)** is a rigorously defined, iterative, and end-to-end procedural framework that orchestrates the systematic transformation of raw, heterogeneous, and often unstructured data into actionable business intelligence, predictive insights, and prescriptive decisions. In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme and NEP 2020 outcome-based education model, the DALC represents the canonical sequence of engineering activities that convert a vague organizational problem statement into a deployed, monitored, and value-generating analytical solution.

The life cycle is **iterative** (each phase can trigger revisits to prior phases), **adaptive** (applicable across batch, real-time, and streaming analytics), and **discipline-agnostic** (it generalizes to any domain — finance, healthcare, manufacturing, e-commerce, or IoT). It is the structural backbone upon which enterprise-grade analytics platforms (Hadoop, Spark, Snowflake, Azure Synapse) are operationalized.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — PECAT864 Module 2:**
> Students are expected to articulate the phases of the Data Analytics Life Cycle, distinguish between the three primary discovery methods (Hypothesis-driven, Data-driven, Hybrid), and map each life-cycle phase to its corresponding analytical activity, governance requirement, and Big Data toolset.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Intuitive "House Building" Model

Imagine the Data Analytics Life Cycle as the **process of constructing a custom house** for a client:

| DALC Phase | House-Building Analogy | Intuitive Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Phase 1: Discovery** | Meeting the client to understand their needs, budget, and lifestyle | Define *what* problem we are solving and *why* |
| **Phase 2: Data Preparation** | Surveying the land, clearing debris, sourcing quality materials | Acquire, clean, and structure the raw data |
| **Phase 3: Model Planning** | Drafting the architectural blueprint | Choose algorithms, statistical methods, and feature engineering |
| **Phase 4: Model Building** | Actual brick-laying, plumbing, and electrical work | Train the analytical model on prepared data |
| **Phase 5: Communication** | Walk-through with the client, presenting the finished house | Translate technical results into business narratives |
| **Phase 6: Operationalization** | Handing over the keys, occupancy permit, ongoing maintenance | Deploy, monitor, and continuously improve the model |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the Life Cycle "Iterative"?** Just as a homeowner might request a kitchen renovation after moving in, an analyst often discovers new data patterns that require returning to the preparation or modeling phase. The life cycle is a **loop**, not a one-way ladder.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Anchors

- **Big Data Characteristics (5 V's):** **Volume** (scale of data), **Velocity** (speed of generation), **Variety** (structural heterogeneity), **Veracity** (data trustworthiness), and **Value** (insight density).
- **Hypothesis-Driven Discovery:** Starts with a pre-defined business question; data is collected to *test* the hypothesis.
- **Data-Driven Discovery:** No pre-formed hypothesis; algorithms autonomously uncover patterns (e.g., clustering, anomaly detection).
- **Hybrid Discovery:** Combines both — a hypothesis guides exploration, but data-driven techniques refine it.

> [!TIP]
> The Data Analytics Life Cycle is **industry-standard** but **vendor-flexible**. Major variants include the **EMC/DASCA Framework** (6 phases), the **Microsoft Team Data Science Process (TDSP)** (5 stages), and the **IBM Data First Methodology** (4 stages). The EMC version is the most frequently tested in KTU examinations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Canonical Phases (EMC/DASCA Framework — KTU Reference Standard)

The Data Analytics Life Cycle, as adopted by the EMC Data Science curriculum and widely referenced in KTU board examinations, comprises the following six phases:

### Phase 1 — Discovery (Problem Framing & Knowledge Acquisition)
- **Objective:** Define the business problem, identify stakeholders, and establish success criteria.
- **Key Activities:**
  - Formulating the *business problem statement* (e.g., "Reduce customer churn by 15% in Q1").
  - Identifying data sources (transactional systems, logs, social media, IoT sensors).
  - Conducting a **data feasibility study** — is the data available, accessible, and sufficient?
  - Framing initial hypotheses.
- **KTU Tip:** This phase is where the **SMART criteria** (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound) are applied to the analytical objective.

### Phase 2 — Data Preparation (Acquisition, Cleansing, Transformation)
- **Objective:** Transform raw data into a clean, integrated, and analysis-ready dataset.
- **Key Activities:**
  - **Data ingestion** from heterogeneous sources (RDBMS, NoSQL, APIs, streaming platforms like Kafka).
  - **Data cleansing:** handling missing values, removing duplicates, correcting inconsistencies.
  - **Data transformation:** normalization, standardization, encoding categorical variables.
  - **Data integration:** schema reconciliation across sources.
  - **Feature engineering:** creating derived variables that improve model performance.
- **KTU Tip:** This is the **most time-consuming phase** in practice — industry benchmarks estimate it consumes **60–80%** of an analyst's effort.

### Phase 3 — Model Planning (Analytical Strategy & Methodology Selection)
- **Objective:** Select the most appropriate statistical, machine learning, or deep learning techniques.
- **Key Activities:**
  - Choosing model families (regression, classification, clustering, recommendation).
  - Performing **Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)** — descriptive statistics, correlation analysis, visualization.
  - Designing the experimental setup — training, validation, and test splits.
  - Selecting evaluation metrics (RMSE, F1-score, AUC-ROC, Silhouette Score).
- **KTU Tip:** EDA tools (Pandas, Matplotlib, Seaborn, Tableau) are integral to this phase.

### Phase 4 — Model Building (Training, Validation, Hyperparameter Tuning)
- **Objective:** Develop the analytical model that best fits the prepared data and satisfies the business objective.
- **Key Activities:**
  - Training candidate models (e.g., Linear Regression, Random Forest, XGBoost, Neural Networks).
  - **Cross-validation** (typically k-fold with $k = 5$ or $k = 10$) to estimate generalization performance.
  - **Hyperparameter optimization** via Grid Search, Random Search, or Bayesian Optimization.
  - **Model ensembling** (Bagging, Boosting, Stacking) when a single model underperforms.
- **KTU Tip:** The **Bias-Variance Tradeoff** is the central conceptual pillar of this phase.

### Phase 5 — Communication of Results (Insight Synthesis & Stakeholder Engagement)
- **Objective:** Deliver findings in a manner that is both technically rigorous and business-actionable.
- **Key Activities:**
  - Translating statistical outputs into **business narratives** (e.g., "A 1-unit increase in X yields a 12% lift in Y").
  - Creating executive dashboards (Tableau, Power BI, Looker).
  - Documenting assumptions, limitations, and confidence intervals.
  - **Storytelling with data** — using the pyramid principle to structure findings.

### Phase 6 — Operationalization (Deployment, Monitoring, Maintenance)
- **Objective:** Embed the validated model into production systems and ensure sustained business value.
- **Key Activities:**
  - Deploying models as **REST APIs**, batch jobs, or embedded services (Docker, Kubernetes).
  - Implementing **CI/CD pipelines** for analytics (ModelOps / MLOps).
  - **Model monitoring:** tracking data drift, concept drift, and prediction quality.
  - Establishing **retraining schedules** and feedback loops.
  - Governance: ensuring **GDPR**, **HIPAA**, and **ethical AI** compliance.

## 2.2 Three Discovery Methods — A Comparative Framework

| Method | Starting Point | Best Use Case | Risk Profile |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hypothesis-Driven** | Pre-defined business question | A/B testing, clinical trials, root-cause analysis | Misses serendipitous discoveries |
| **Data-Driven** | The data itself | Customer segmentation, fraud detection, anomaly discovery | Generates many false leads |
| **Hybrid** | Business question + exploratory data mining | Churn prediction, recommendation engines | Requires skilled analysts to balance both |

## 2.3 Cross-Framework Comparison (KTU High-Yield)

| Framework | # of Phases | Naming Convention | Industry Origin |
|---|---|---|---|
| **EMC / DASCA** | 6 | Discovery, Prep, Planning, Building, Communicate, Operate | EMC Education |
| **Microsoft TDSP** | 5 | Business Understanding, Data Acquisition, Modeling, Deployment, Customer Acceptance | Microsoft Azure |
| **IBM Data First** | 4 | Define, Collect, Analyze, Act | IBM Analytics |
| **CRISP-DM** | 6 | Business Understanding, Data Understanding, Data Prep, Modeling, Evaluation, Deployment | Cross-industry (1996) |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRISP-DM (Cross-Industry Standard Process for Data Mining)** is the oldest and most universally adopted framework. While not the KTU primary reference, it is frequently cited as a comparison point. KTU 2024 examiners expect students to know its phase names and contrast it with the EMC framework.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Definition | Purpose / KTU Application |
|---|---|---|
| Dataset Split Ratio | $\text{Ratio} = \frac{\vert \text{Train} \vert}{\vert \text{Validation} \vert : \vert \text{Test} \vert}$ | Typically 70:15:15 or 80:10:10 |
| k-Fold Cross-Validation Error | $E_{CV} = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} L(y_i, \hat{y}_i^{(k)})$ | Robust generalization estimate |
| Bias-Variance Decomposition | $\mathbb{E}\left[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2\right] = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$ | Model complexity tuning |
| F1-Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ where $P = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}$, $R = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | Balanced classification metric |
| AUC-ROC | $\text{AUC} = \int_0^1 \text{TPR}(\text{FPR}^{-1}(t)) \, dt$ | Threshold-independent classifier quality |
| RMSE | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}$ | Regression error metric |
| Silhouette Score | $s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}$ | Cluster cohesion & separation |
| Time-in-Phase (Industry Benchmark) | $\text{Discovery} : \text{Prep} : \text{Plan} : \text{Build} : \text{Comm} : \text{Op} \approx 5:70:10:5:5:5$ | Effort allocation reality |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Data Analytics Life Cycle is the operational blueprint for:

- **Healthcare Analytics:** Predicting patient readmission, drug efficacy modeling, and clinical pathway optimization.
- **Financial Services:** Credit scoring, algorithmic trading, real-time fraud detection.
- **Smart Manufacturing:** Predictive maintenance, quality control via computer vision, and supply-chain optimization.
- **Smart Cities:** Traffic flow optimization, energy grid balancing, and pollution forecasting.
- **Retail & E-Commerce:** Recommendation systems, dynamic pricing, and customer lifetime value (CLV) prediction.

> [!TIP]
> The DALC is **tool-agnostic**. The same life cycle can be executed using a Python + Scikit-learn stack, an R + Tidyverse stack, or a cloud-native pipeline (AWS SageMaker, Azure ML, GCP Vertex AI). The KTU examiner will accept any standard tool naming as long as the *phase* is correctly identified.

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Worked Derivations

## 3.1 Worked Example — Mapping a Real Problem to the DALC

**Problem Statement:** A Kerala-based online grocery startup wants to reduce customer churn by 20% over the next quarter using analytics on transactional and behavioral data.

### Step-by-Phase Mapping:

**Phase 1 — Discovery:**
- **Business Objective:** Reduce monthly churn rate from 12% to ≤ 9.6%.
- **Stakeholders Identified:** Marketing Head, Data Engineering Lead, CFO.
- **Data Sources Surveyed:** CRM (customer demographics), order logs (RDBMS), web/app clickstream (Kafka streams), customer service tickets (NoSQL).
- **Hypothesis Formulated:** "Customers who experience ≥ 2 delivery delays and have not used the loyalty program in 30 days are 3× more likely to churn."

**Phase 2 — Data Preparation:**
- Ingest 18 months of historical data (~4.2 TB) from PostgreSQL, MongoDB, and Kafka.
- Handle missing values in `customer_age` (3.1% missing) using **median imputation**.
- Remove duplicate transactions (≈ 0.4% of records).
- Engineer features: `avg_delivery_delay`, `loyalty_engagement_score`, `purchase_frequency_30d`, `cart_abandonment_rate`.
- Output: a clean, feature-engineered analytical table in Parquet format.

**Phase 3 — Model Planning:**
- **Task type:** Binary classification (Churn = 1, Retained = 0).
- **Candidate algorithms:** Logistic Regression, Random Forest, XGBoost, LightGBM.
- **Evaluation metric selected:** F1-Score (justified due to class imbalance — churners are only 12% of base).
- **Split strategy:** Stratified 80:20 train-test split to preserve class distribution.

**Phase 4 — Model Building:**

Let the trained model be denoted $\hat{f}(X)$, where $X$ is the feature matrix. The cross-validation error is computed as:

$$E_{CV} = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} L\left(y_i, \hat{f}^{-k(i)}(x_i)\right)$$

where $\hat{f}^{-k(i)}$ is the model trained on all folds except the $i$-th, and $L(\cdot, \cdot)$ is the loss function (e.g., log-loss for classification).

For $k = 5$ and using F1 as the metric:

$$\text{F1}_{CV} = \frac{1}{5} \sum_{i=1}^{5} F_1\left(y^{(i)}, \hat{y}^{(i)}\right)$$

The team selects **XGBoost** after tuning hyperparameters ($\text{max\_depth} = 6$, $\text{learning\_rate} = 0.05$, $n_{\text{estimators}} = 300$) via Bayesian Optimization, achieving:

$$\text{F1}_{CV} = 0.78, \quad \text{AUC-ROC} = 0.84$$

**Phase 5 — Communication of Results:**

- The model identifies the top-3 churn drivers via SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) values:
  1. `avg_delivery_delay` (importance = 0.31)
  2. `loyalty_engagement_score` (importance = 0.27)
  3. `cart_abandonment_rate` (importance = 0.19)
- Presented via a Tableau dashboard with cohort drill-downs.
- Translated to business narrative: *"For every 1-day reduction in average delivery delay, projected churn drops by 4.2%."*

**Phase 6 — Operationalization:**
- Model serialized via `joblib` and deployed as a **Docker container** behind a **FastAPI** endpoint on Kubernetes.
- **Daily batch scoring** of all active customers; top-10% churn-risk customers pushed to the CRM for targeted retention campaigns.
- Monitoring: **data drift detection** via the Population Stability Index (PSI):

$$\text{PSI} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (A_i - B_i) \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{A_i}{B_i}\right)$$

where $A_i$ and $B_i$ are the proportions of observations in bin $i$ for the training and live data, respectively.

- **Action threshold:** If $\text{PSI} > 0.2$, trigger model retraining.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Phase × Activity × Tool Mapping

| Phase | Primary Activity | Output Artifact | Representative Tools |
|---|---|---|---|
| Discovery | Stakeholder interviews, KPI definition | Business Requirements Document | Miro, Confluence, JIRA |
| Data Preparation | ETL, cleansing, feature engineering | Curated analytical dataset | Spark, Hive, dbt, Airflow |
| Model Planning | EDA, algorithm selection | Experimental design document | Pandas, Matplotlib, Scikit-learn |
| Model Building | Training, validation, tuning | Trained model artifact | XGBoost, TensorFlow, PyTorch |
| Communication | Visualization, storytelling | Executive dashboard & report | Tableau, Power BI, Streamlit |
| Operationalization | Deployment, monitoring | Production API + monitoring dashboard | Docker, Kubernetes, MLflow, Airflow |

## 3.3 Effort Distribution Analysis (Industry Standard)

Empirical studies (Anaconda 2022 State of Data Science Report) indicate the following effort distribution across the life cycle:

$$\text{Effort}_{\text{Discovery}} \approx 5\%$$
$$\text{Effort}_{\text{Data Preparation}} \approx 70\%$$
$$\text{Effort}_{\text{Model Planning}} \approx 10\%$$
$$\text{Effort}_{\text{Model Building}} \approx 5\%$$
$$\text{Effort}_{\text{Communication}} \approx 5\%$$
$$\text{Effort}_{\text{Operationalization}} \approx 5\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The sum must equal $100\%$. This distribution is a **frequently tested KTU concept** — examiners expect students to articulate that data preparation is disproportionately effort-intensive due to the **3 V's challenge** of Big Data (Volume, Velocity, Variety).

## 3.4 Why the Life Cycle is Iterative — Formal Justification

The life cycle is not strictly linear because each phase can reveal information that invalidates prior assumptions. Formally, let the state after phase $i$ be denoted $S_i$. In a linear (waterfall) model:

$$S_{i+1} = f_i(S_i)$$

In the **iterative DALC model**, the state can revert:

$$S_{i} \leftarrow f_{i-1}(S_i) \quad \text{if} \quad \text{Validation}(S_i) = \text{FAIL}$$

This is why the DALC is best represented as a **cyclic directed graph** (see Section 4) rather than a linear pipeline.
