- **Data Sources:** Transactional DBs, Web Logs, IoT Sensors, Social Media, Mobile Apps.
- **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
  - $D(t) = D_0 \cdot e^{k t}$ — Exponential data growth.
  - $S(p) = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{p}}$ — Amdahl's Law.
  - $E_p = \dfrac{S_p}{p}$ — Parallel efficiency.
  - $CR = \dfrac{\text{Original}}{\text{Compressed}}$ — Compression ratio.
  - $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ — F1-Score.
  - $R^2 = 1 - \dfrac{SS_{\text{res}}}{SS_{\text{tot}}}$ — Model fit.
- **Storage units:** TB = $10^{12}$, PB = $10^{15}$, EB = $10^{18}$, ZB = $10^{21}$ bytes.
- **Era Timeline:** File Reports (1960s) → DW (1990s) → BI (2000s) → Big Data (2010s) → Cloud+AI (2015s+) → LLMs/Edge (2020s+).
- **The Ceiling of Parallelism:** Amdahl's Law shows speedup is bounded by $\dfrac{1}{1-f}$. Serial fractions can never be eliminated completely.
- **Real-world examples (remember at least 3):** Amazon recommendations, Google Search ranking, UPI fraud detection, hospital predictive diagnostics, Netflix content optimization, Tesla autonomous driving.
- **One-line conclusion (for 14-mark answers):** *"The state of practice in analytics is a dynamic convergence of disciplines, roles, and platforms that has shifted organizations from retrospective reporting toward real-time, AI-driven, prescriptive decision-making — with Big Data as its fuel."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Example Applications - Credit Risk Modeling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECAT864 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Example Applications - Credit Risk Modeling # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Credit Risk Modeling** is a quantitative discipline that employs statistical, mathematical, and machine learning techniques on large-scale historical and real-time data to estimate the **probability that a borrower (individual or institution) will fail to meet its contractual debt obligations**, and to quantify the resulting financial exposure to the lending institution. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context of Big Data Analytics, credit risk modeling extends classical banking analytics by ingesting **high-volume, high-velocity, and high-variety** data sources (transactional streams, mobile usage, social signals, alternative data) into scalable analytics pipelines (Hadoop, Spark, NoSQL) to produce real-time, granular, and more accurate risk scores than traditional scorecard approaches. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PECAT864 – Module 2):** Credit Risk Modeling is presented as a flagship *example application* of big data analytics where predictive modeling, large-scale feature engineering, and streaming data converge. Expect questions framed around *why big data improves credit scoring* and *how the PD–LGD–EAD framework is implemented on distributed systems*. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are a **village money-lender** deciding whether to lend ₹50,000 to a stranger. You cannot rely on paperwork; you observe their behavior: - Do they own land or a stable job? - Have they repaid previous small loans punctually? - Do they spend extravagantly or live within means? - Are neighbours and shopkeepers trustworthy of them? You mentally weigh these cues and assign a "trust score." If the score is low, you charge higher interest or refuse the loan entirely. **Credit Risk Modeling is the algorithmic, data-driven, and massively scaled version of this same intuition.** Instead of one money-lender assessing one borrower, a bank assesses **millions** of borrowers in milliseconds using thousands of features, mathematical models, and big data infrastructure. > [!NOTE] > **Core Industry Metrics (must memorize for KTU):** > - **PD (Probability of Default)** — likelihood borrower defaults within 1 year. > - **LGD (Loss Given Default)** — fraction of exposure unrecovered post-default, expressed in **%**. > - **EAD (Exposure at Default)** — total outstanding amount at the moment of default. > - **EL (Expected Loss)** — anticipated monetary loss, computed as **PD × LGD × EAD**. > - **Unexpected Loss (UL)** — the volatility around EL, covered by **economic capital**. ## 1.3 Why Big Data is a Game-Changer for Credit Risk Traditional scorecards (e.g., classic FICO) used only **15–25 variables** from credit bureaus. Big data enables: | Dimension | Traditional | Big Data Enhanced | |---|---|---| | Data Volume | Thousands of records | Billions of events | | Data Variety | Bureau data only | Mobile, social, telco, e-commerce, psychometrics | | Velocity | Batch (monthly) | Real-time streaming | | Model Type | Logistic regression scorecard | Ensemble ML, Deep Learning, Graph-based | | Inclusion | "Thin-file" customers excluded | "Thin-file" customers scored via alternative data | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Credit Risk (Basel Framework) The **Basel II/III Accord** formalizes credit risk through three measurable quantities. Big data analytics improves the estimation accuracy of each. ### Pillar 1 — Probability of Default (PD) - Definition: Likelihood that a borrower defaults within a defined horizon (commonly **12 months**). - Range: **0 ≤ PD ≤ 1**. - Big Data enhancement: PD is no longer just a static score; it becomes a **dynamic, time-varying probability** updated as new transactions stream in. ### Pillar 2 — Loss Given Default (LGD) - Definition: Proportion of the exposure that is *lost* after default, net of recoveries and collateral. - Range: **0% ≤ LGD ≤ 100%**. - Big Data enhancement: Real-time collateral valuation using web-scraped property prices, market indices, and asset depreciation curves. ### Pillar 3 — Exposure at Default (EAD) - Definition: The total outstanding obligation (principal + accrued interest + unused credit lines drawn) at the moment of default. - Big Data enhancement: Credit conversion factors (CCF) are re-estimated continuously using behavioral transaction streams. ## 2.2 Theoretical Pipeline — How a Big Data Credit Model Works The operational logic can be broken into six structured steps: 1. **Data Ingestion Layer:** Streaming sources (Kafka, Flume) and batch sources (HDFS, S3) feed raw events — payments, mobile top-ups, clicks, geolocation. 2. **Data Lake / Warehouse:** Raw data lands in a schema-on-read storage (HDFS, BigQuery, Cassandra) preserving original fidelity. 3. **Feature Engineering:** Transformation into model-ready features using Spark, Hive, or Pandas-on-Spark. Common engineered features: - **Behavioural aggregates** (rolling 30-day spend, payment volatility). - **Velocity features** (transaction count per hour). - **Graph features** (PageRank of borrower's transaction network — fraud rings). - **WoE / IV bins** (Weight of Evidence, Information Value — classical but still used). 4. **Model Training Layer:** Distributed training on Spark MLlib, H2O, or TensorFlow-on-Spark. 5. **Validation & Calibration:** Out-of-time back-testing, Population Stability Index (PSI), Gini, AUC. 6. **Scoring & Decision Engine:** Real-time scoring produces PD, which is mapped to a credit decision (approve / reject / limit / price). ## 2.3 Why Each Step Matters (The "Why") - **Why feature engineering dominates?** — In credit risk, well-engineered features (e.g., payment-to-income ratio) outperform fancy algorithms. Garbage features → garbage PD. - **Why distributed training?** — Datasets exceed RAM of a single node; algorithms must scale horizontally. - **Why back-testing out-of-time?** — Credit crises (2008, 2020) reveal that in-sample PDs grossly underestimate tail risk. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table is **exam-critical**. Memorize every entry, including boundary values, for KTU 2024 Scheme ESE and internal assessments. | # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Range / Units | KTU Significance | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Expected Loss | $EL = PD \times LGD \times EAD$ | Currency units (₹, $, etc.) | Direct 3–7 mark question | | 2 | Unexpected Loss (99.9% VaR) | $UL = EAD \times \sqrt{LGD^2 \cdot \sigma_{PD}^2 + PD \cdot \sigma_{LGD}^2}$ | Currency units | Capital adequacy | | 3 | Logit (Log-Odds) Link | $\text{logit}(p) = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{p}{1-p}\right)$ | Real number | Foundation of PD model | | 4 | Logistic Regression Score | $p = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-(\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \dots + \beta_n x_n)}}$ | $(0, 1)$ | Standard classifier | | 5 | Odds Ratio | $OR = \dfrac{p}{1-p}$ | $(0, \infty)$ | Interpretability | | 6 | Weight of Evidence (WoE) | $WoE_i = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{\text{Dist. of Goods in bin }i}{\text{Dist. of Bads in bin }i}\right)$ | Real number | Feature transformation | | 7 | Information Value (IV) | $IV = \sum_{i=1}^{k} (G_i - B_i) \times \ln\!\left(\dfrac{G_i}{B_i}\right)$ | Real number | Feature selection rule | | 8 | Gini Coefficient | $Gini = 2 \cdot AUC - 1$ | $[-1, 1]$, ideally $\geq 0.4$ | Model discrimination | | 9 | Kolmogorov–Smirnov (KS) | $KS = \max_t \;\vert \; F_{good}(t) - F_{bad}(t) \;\vert$ | $[0, 1]$, ideally $\geq 0.3$ | Model ranking power | | 10 | Population Stability Index | $PSI = \sum_{i=1}^{k} (A_i - E_i) \times \ln\!\left(\dfrac{A_i}{E_i}\right)$ | $[0, \infty)$, alert if $\geq 0.25$ | Model drift detection | | 11 | Conversion Factor (CCF) | $CCF = \dfrac{\text{Drawn at default} - \text{Drawn at observation}}{\text{Limit} - \text{Drawn at observation}}$ | $[0, 1]$ | EAD estimation | | 12 | Recovery Rate | $RR = 1 - LGD$ | $[0, 1]$ | Inverse of LGD | **IV Interpretation Rule (memorize):** | IV Range | Predictive Power | |---|---| | $< 0.02$ | Useless | | $0.02$ to $0.1$ | Weak | | $0.1$ to $0.3$ | Medium | | $0.3$ to $0.5$ | Strong | | $> 0.5$ | Suspicious — over-predicting | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Credit risk models built on big data platforms are used by: - **Retail banks** (HDFC, ICICI, JPMorgan) for instant personal-loan approvals on mobile apps. - **Fintech lenders** (PaySense, KreditBee) where thin-file customers are scored using mobile metadata. - **Buy-Now-Pay-Later (BNPL)** firms for real-time micro-credit decisions at point-of-sale. - **Supply chain finance** where graph analytics detect distressed counterparties. - **Regulators (RBI, Basel Committee)** for systemic-risk monitoring using network-level features. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Logistic Regression Credit Scorecard The most widely used PD model is the **binary logistic regression** (Good = 0, Bad = 1). Below is the complete mathematical derivation. ### Step 1 — Start with the Odds The **odds** of default measure how likely default is versus non-default for any feature vector $\mathbf{x}$. $$ \text{Odds}(\mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{P(Y = 1 \mid \mathbf{x})}{P(Y = 0 \mid \mathbf{x})} = \dfrac{p(\mathbf{x})}{1 - p(\mathbf{x})} $$ ### Step 2 — Take the Natural Logarithm (Logit Transform) We model the **log-odds** as a linear combination of input features because probabilities must lie in $(0, 1)$, but linear functions are unbounded. $$ \text{logit}(p(\mathbf{x})) = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{p(\mathbf{x})}{1 - p(\mathbf{x})}\right) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \dots + \beta_n x_n $$ *Conversion Logic:* The logit transformation maps the bounded probability interval $(0, 1)$ to the entire real line $(-\infty, \infty)$, which is exactly the range of a linear function. ### Step 3 — Invert the Logit to Recover Probability Exponentiate both sides: $$ \dfrac{p(\mathbf{x})}{1 - p(\mathbf{x})} = e^{\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \dots + \beta_n x_n} $$ Solve for $p(\mathbf{x})$: $$ p(\mathbf{x}) = e^{\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \dots + \beta_n x_n} \cdot \bigl(1 - p(\mathbf{x})\bigr) $$ $$ p(\mathbf{x}) \cdot \bigl(1 + e^{\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \dots + \beta_n x_n}\bigr) = e^{\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \dots + \beta_n x_n} $$ $$ p(\mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{e^{\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \dots + \beta_n x_n}}{1 + e^{\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \dots + \beta_n x_n}} $$ Divide numerator and denominator by the exponential to obtain the canonical form: $$ p(\mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-(\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \dots + \beta_n x_n)}} $$ This is the **sigmoid function** applied to a linear combination of features. ### Step 4 — Parameter Estimation via Maximum Likelihood Given a dataset of $N$ borrowers with outcomes $y_i \in \{0, 1\}$, the **likelihood** is: $$ L(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} p(\mathbf{x}_i)^{y_i} \bigl(1 - p(\mathbf{x}_i)\bigr)^{1 - y_i} $$ The **log-likelihood** (easier to maximize) is: $$ \ell(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Bigl[ y_i \ln p(\mathbf{x}_i) + (1 - y_i) \ln\bigl(1 - p(\mathbf{x}_i)\bigr) \Bigr] $$ The MLE estimate $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$ is found by setting $\partial \ell / \partial \beta_j = 0$ for all $j$, typically solved iteratively using **Newton–Raphson** or **Iteratively Reweighted Least Squares (IRLS)** — distributed via Spark MLlib's `LogisticRegression` for big data. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Expected Loss Calculation **Problem Statement:** A bank has a portfolio of **10,000 personal loans**. Historical analysis on big data streams yields: - Average PD = **4.2%** - Average LGD = **55%** - Average EAD per loan = **₹1,50,000** **Compute:** ### Step 1 — Portfolio-Level Expected Loss $$ EL_{\text{portfolio}} = N \times PD \times LGD \times EAD $$ $$ EL_{\text{portfolio}} = 10{,}000 \times 0.042 \times 0.55 \times 1{,}50{,}000 $$ $$ EL_{\text{portfolio}} = 10{,}000 \times 0.042 \times 82{,}500 $$ $$ EL_{\text{portfolio}} = 10{,}000 \times 3{,}465 = \text{₹}3{,}46{,}50{,}000 $$ $$ \boxed{EL_{\text{portfolio}} = \text{₹}3.465 \text{ Crores}} $$ ### Step 2 — Per-Loan Expected Loss (Useful for Pricing) $$ EL_{\text{loan}} = 0.042 \times 0.55 \times 1{,}50{,}000 = \text{₹}3{,}465 $$ ### Step 3 — Risk-Adjusted Pricing If the bank wants a **2% return on risk** (profit margin over expected loss), minimum interest charged per loan is: $$ \text{Interest floor} = \dfrac{EL_{\text{loan}}}{\text{EAD}} + \text{Operating cost} + \text{Profit margin} $$ $$ \text{Interest floor} = 0.0231 + 0.015 + 0.02 = 0.0581 \;\;(5.81\%) $$ So the loan should be priced at a minimum of **5.81% per annum** to remain profitable on a risk-adjusted basis. ## 3.3 Derivation of the Information Value (IV) Statistic ### Step 1 — Discretize Feature into $k$ Bins For a categorical (or binned continuous) feature, count the number of goods ($G_i$) and bads ($B_i$) in bin $i$. ### Step 2 — Compute Distribution Percentages $$ g_i = \dfrac{G_i}{G_{\text{total}}}, \qquad b_i = \dfrac{B_i}{B_{\text{total}}} $$ ### Step 3 — Compute Per-Bin Contribution $$ IV_i = (g_i - b_i) \times \ln\!\left(\dfrac{g_i}{b_i}\right) $$ *Conversion Logic:* This expression is derived from the **Kullback–Leibler divergence** between the good and bad distributions — it measures how much information bin $i$ carries about discriminating goods from bads. ### Step 4 — Sum Across All Bins $$ IV = \sum_{i=1}^{k} IV_i $$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Model Trade-offs in Big Data Credit Risk | Model | Strength | Weakness | When to Use (KTU Context) | |---|---|---|---| | Logistic Regression | Interpretable, regulator-friendly | Limited non-linearity | Baseline scorecard | | Random Forest | Handles non-linearity, robust | Less interpretable | Cross-selling risk | | Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) | State-of-the-art accuracy | Risk of overfitting | Default prediction | | Neural Networks | Captures complex patterns | Black-box, data-hungry | Alternative-data scoring | | Graph Neural Networks | Detects fraud rings | Computationally expensive | Anti-fraud + credit | | Survival Models (Cox) | Models time-to-default | Assumes proportional hazards | LGD/EAD modeling | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End Big Data Credit Risk Modeling Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SRC["DATA SOURCES LAYER"] S1["Transactional Streams
Kafka Topic payments"] S2["Mobile CDR
Call Detail Records"] S3["Social Media
Twitter LinkedIn"] S4["Credit Bureau
CIBIL Experian"] S5["Open Banking APIs
Account Aggregator"] end subgraph ING["INGESTION LAYER"] I1["Kafka Broker"] I2["Flume Agent"] I3["Sqoop Loader"] end subgraph STORE["STORAGE LAYER"] ST1["HDFS Data Lake
Raw Zone"] ST2["HBase Cassandra
Real time ODS"] ST3["Hive Warehouse
Curated Zone"] end subgraph PROC["PROCESSING LAYER"] P1["Spark Streaming
Micro batch ETL"] P2["Spark MLlib
Feature Engineering"] P3["H2O Spark
Model Training"] P4["Airflow
Orchestration"] end subgraph MODEL["MODELING LAYER"] M1["Logistic Regression
Baseline PD"] M2["XGBoost
Production PD"] M3["LGD Survival Model"] M4["EAD Regression Model"] end subgraph SCORE["SCORING LAYER"] SC1["Real time Scoring API
Sub 100 ms latency"] SC2["Batch Scoring
Daily refresh"] SC3["Model Monitoring
PSI AUC drift"] end subgraph CONS["CONSUMPTION LAYER"] C1["Loan Origination System"] C2["Collection Strategy Engine"] C3["Regulatory Reporting
RBI Basel III"] C4["Management Dashboard"] end S1 --> I1 S2 --> I1 S3 --> I2 S4 --> I3 S5 --> I1 I1 --> ST1 I2 --> ST1 I3 --> ST1 ST1 --> P1 ST2 --> P1 P1 --> ST3 ST3 --> P2 P2 --> P3 P4 --> P1 P4 --> P3 P3 --> M1 P3 --> M2 P3 --> M3 P3 --> M4 M1 --> SC1 M2 --> SC1 M3 --> SC2 M4 --> SC2 SC1 --> SC3 SC2 --> SC3 SC1 --> C1 SC2 --> C2 SC3 --> C3 SC3 --> C4 ``` ## 4.2 Feature Engineering & Selection Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Raw Customer Data"] --> B["Data Cleaning
Missing Outlier"] B --> C["Binning
Optimal cuts"] C --> D["WoE Transformation"] D --> E["IV Computation
Feature Ranking"] E --> F{IV Greater 0.02} F -- Yes --> G["Keep Feature"] F -- No --> H["Discard Feature"] G --> I["Correlation Filter
VIF Less 5"] I --> J["Final Feature Set
Model Ready"] ``` ## 4.3 Model Validation Cycle ```mermaid flowchart TB M["Trained Model"] --> V1["Discrimination
Gini AUC KS"] M --> V2["Calibration
Hosmer Lemeshow"] M --> V3["Stability
PSI CSI"] M --> V4["Back testing
Out of time"] V1 --> G{"All Metrics Pass"} V2 --> G V3 --> G V4 --> G G -- Yes --> DEP["Deploy to Production"] G -- No --> RET["Retrain or Reject"] DEP --> MON["Continuous Monitoring"] MON --> DRIFT{"Drift Detected"} DRIFT -- Yes --> RET DRIFT -- No --> MON ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** **Define Probability of Default (PD) and Loss Given Default (LGD). How does big data analytics improve the estimation of PD compared to traditional scorecards?** **Model Answer (Examiner's Key):** - **PD** is the likelihood that a borrower will default on a contractual obligation within a specified time horizon, typically 12 months. Mathematically, $PD \in [0, 1]$. - **LGD** is the proportion of exposure that is lost if default occurs, expressed as a percentage, $LGD \in [0\%, 100\%]$. - **Big Data improvement:** Traditional scorecards use 15–25 bureau features updated monthly. Big data adds *real-time transactional signals, mobile metadata, social graphs,* and *alternative data*, enabling PD to be re-estimated continuously with thousands of features rather than a static snapshot. - **(3 marks awarded for: PD definition 1M, LGD definition 1M, big data improvement 1M.)** ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **List any three big data technologies used in the credit risk modeling pipeline and state one specific role of each.** **Model Answer (Examiner's Key):** 1. **Apache Kafka** — Real-time ingestion of transaction streams into the data lake. **(1 mark)** 2. **Apache Spark MLlib** — Distributed training of PD models on billions of records using logistic regression or gradient boosting. **(1 mark)** 3. **Apache HBase / Cassandra** — Low-latency storage of customer profiles for real-time scoring via REST APIs. **(1 mark)** *(Acceptable alternatives: Hadoop HDFS for storage, Flume for ingestion, Hive for warehousing, Airflow for orchestration, TensorFlow-on-Spark for deep learning.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### **Question A — [14 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** **(a)** Derive the logistic regression equation used in PD modeling starting from the odds of default. Clearly show the sigmoid transformation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A bank's portfolio of 5,000 home loans has the following risk metrics: average PD = 3.5%, average LGD = 60%, average EAD = ₹25,00,000. Compute the total Expected Loss (EL) of the portfolio and the risk-adjusted minimum interest rate if the bank's operating cost is 1.2% and desired profit margin is 1.5%. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution** **Part (a) — Derivation** Step 1 — Define the odds of default as the ratio of the probability of default to the probability of non-default: $$ \text{Odds} = \dfrac{p}{1-p} $$ Step 2 — Apply the natural logarithm to obtain the logit function, which transforms a probability in $(0, 1)$ to the entire real line $(-\infty, +\infty)$: $$ \text{logit}(p) = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{p}{1-p}\right) $$ Step 3 — Model the logit as a linear combination of borrower features $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n$ with coefficients $\beta_0, \beta_1, \dots, \beta_n$: $$ \ln\!\left(\dfrac{p(\mathbf{x})}{1-p(\mathbf{x})}\right) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \dots + \beta_n x_n $$ Step 4 — Exponentiate both sides: $$ \dfrac{p(\mathbf{x})}{1-p(\mathbf{x})} = e^{\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \dots + \beta_n x_n} $$ Step 5 — Solve algebraically for $p(\mathbf{x})$: $$ p(\mathbf{x}) = e^{\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \dots + \beta_n x_n} - p(\mathbf{x}) \cdot e^{\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \dots + \beta_n x_n} $$ $$ p(\mathbf{x}) \bigl[1 + e^{\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \dots + \beta_n x_n}\bigr] = e^{\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \dots + \beta_n x_n} $$ $$ \boxed{p(\mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-(\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \dots + \beta_n x_n)}}} $$ This is the **sigmoid (logistic) function** evaluated at the linear score, mapping any real-valued linear combination into a probability between 0 and 1. **(Full 7 marks: odds 1M, logit 1M, linear form 1M, exponentiation 1M, algebraic inversion 2M, sigmoid statement 1M.)** **Part (b) — Numerical Computation** Step 1 — Compute total Expected Loss of the portfolio: $$ EL_{\text{portfolio}} = N \times PD \times LGD \times EAD $$ $$ EL_{\text{portfolio}} = 5{,}000 \times 0.035 \times 0.60 \times 25{,}00{,}000 $$ $$ EL_{\text{portfolio}} = 5{,}000 \times 0.035 \times 15{,}00{,}000 $$ $$ EL_{\text{portfolio}} = 5{,}000 \times 52{,}500 = \text{₹}26{,}25{,}00{,}000 $$ $$ \boxed{EL_{\text{portfolio}} = \text{₹}26.25 \text{ Crores}} $$ Step 2 — Compute per-loan Expected Loss: $$ EL_{\text{loan}} = 0.035 \times 0.60 \times 25{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}52{,}500 $$ Step 3 — Compute the risk premium (expected loss as a fraction of EAD): $$ \text{Risk Premium} = \dfrac{52{,}500}{25{,}00{,}000} = 0.021 = 2.1\% $$ Step 4 — Compute the minimum interest rate (risk premium + operating cost + profit margin): $$ r_{\min} = 2.1\% + 1.2\% + 1.5\% = \boxed{4.8\% \text{ per annum}} $$ **(Full 7 marks: formula 1M, substitution 1M, intermediate calculation 2M, final EL 1M, risk premium 1M, minimum rate 1M.)** --- ### **Question B — [14 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **(a)** Explain the Information Value (IV) and Weight of Evidence (WoE) techniques used in credit risk feature engineering. Give the formula for IV and state the rule for feature selection based on IV ranges. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compute the WoE and IV for the feature "Monthly Income Bracket" given the following table. Comment on the predictive power of the feature. **(7 marks)** | Income Bracket (₹) | # Goods (Non-Default) | # Bads (Default) | |---|---|---| | < 20,000 | 800 | 400 | | 20,000 – 50,000 | 2,400 | 350 | | 50,000 – 1,00,000 | 3,200 | 180 | | > 1,00,000 | 1,600 | 70 | Totals: Goods = 8,000, Bads = 1,000. #### **Model Solution** **Part (a) — Theory** - **WoE** transforms a categorical (or binned continuous) feature into a continuous numerical value that reflects the *strength and direction* of the relationship between the bin and the outcome. $$ WoE_i = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{G_i / G_{\text{total}}}{B_i / B_{\text{total}}}\right) = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{g_i}{b_i}\right) $$ - $WoE > 0$ means the bin has proportionally more goods than bads (lower risk). - $WoE < 0$ means the bin has proportionally more bads than goods (higher risk). - **Information Value (IV)** aggregates the discriminative power of all bins: $$ IV = \sum_{i=1}^{k} (g_i - b_i) \times \ln\!\left(\dfrac{g_i}{b_i}\right) $$ - IV is mathematically equivalent to the **KL-divergence** between good and bad distributions. **Feature Selection Rule (memorize):** | IV Range | Action | |---|---| | IV $< 0.02$ | Useless — discard | | $0.02 \leq IV < 0.1$ | Weak predictor | | $0.1 \leq IV < 0.3$ | Medium predictor | | $0.3 \leq IV \leq 0.5$ | Strong predictor | | $IV > 0.5$ | Suspicious — investigate for over-predicting | **(Full 7 marks: WoE formula 2M, IV formula 2M, interpretation 1M, IV selection rule 2M.)** **Part (b) — Computation** Step 1 — Compute the distribution percentages for each bin: | Income Bracket | $g_i$ | $b_i$ | |---|---|---| | < 20,000 | $800/8000 = 0.1000$ | $400/1000 = 0.4000$ | | 20,000 – 50,000 | $2400/8000 = 0.3000$ | $350/1000 = 0.3500$ | | 50,000 – 1,00,000 | $3200/8000 = 0.4000$ | $180/1000 = 0.1800$ | | > 1,000 | $1600/8000 = 0.2000$ | $70/1000 = 0.0700$ | Step 2 — Compute WoE for each bin: $$ WoE_1 = \ln(0.1000 / 0.4000) = \ln(0.25) = -1.3863 $$ $$ WoE_2 = \ln(0.3000 / 0.3500) = \ln(0.8571) = -0.1542 $$ $$ WoE_3 = \ln(0.4000 / 0.1800) = \ln(2.2222) = +0.7985 $$ $$ WoE_4 = \ln(0.2000 / 0.0700) = \ln(2.8571) = +1.0498 $$ Step 3 — Compute the per-bin IV contribution $(g_i - b_i) \times WoE_i$: $$ IV_1 = (0.1000 - 0.4000) \times (-1.3863) = (-0.3000) \times (-1.3863) = +0.4159 $$ $$ IV_2 = (0.3000 - 0.3500) \times (-0.1542) = (-0.0500) \times (-0.1542) = +0.0077 $$ $$ IV_3 = (0.4000 - 0.1800) \times (+0.7985) = (+0.2200) \times (+0.7985) = +0.1757 $$ $$ IV_4 = (0.2000 - 0.0700) \times (+1.0498) = (+0.1300) \times (+1.0498) = +0.1365 $$ Step 4 — Sum to obtain total IV: $$ IV = 0.4159 + 0.0077 + 0.1757 + 0.1365 = \boxed{0.7358} $$ Step 5 — Interpret: Since $IV = 0.7358 > 0.5$, the feature is **suspiciously strong** — likely *over-predicting*. The bank should investigate for data leakage or sample bias. In practice, the feature "Monthly Income Bracket" is a **medium-to-strong** predictor and should be retained with caution. **(Full 7 marks: distributions 2M, WoE 2M, IV contributions 2M, final IV + interpretation 1M.)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Forgetting the $\mathbf{e^{-(\cdot)}}$ in the sigmoid**: Students often write $p = \frac{e^{(\cdot)}}{1 + e^{(\cdot)}}$ which is also correct — but mixing the two forms within the same derivation costs marks. **Stick to one canonical form.** > 2. **Confusing IV thresholds**: Many students state $IV > 0.5$ as "excellent". It is **not** excellent — it indicates possible leakage and must be flagged. > 3. **Units mismatch in EL**: EAD must be in the **same currency unit** as the final EL. Mixing ₹1,50,000 with EAD in lakhs is a frequent error. > 4. **Skipping back-testing**: When asked "how do you validate a credit model?", stating only AUC/Gini without mentioning **out-of-time back-testing and PSI** results in a 2-mark deduction. > 5. **Ignoring big-data context**: For PECAT864, generic definitions without mentioning **Kafka / Spark / HDFS / distributed training** lose the application-oriented marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Credit Risk** = risk of borrower default; quantified by **PD × LGD × EAD = Expected Loss**. - **PD** is a probability ($0$ to $1$); **LGD** is a percentage ($0\%$ to $100\%$); **EAD** is a currency amount. - **Basel II/III** mandates banks to hold capital for **Unexpected Loss (UL)**, derived from the volatility of PD and LGD. - **Logistic regression** is the *workhorse* of credit scoring — interpretable and regulator-friendly. - **Sigmoid function** $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ is the heart of every credit PD model. - **WoE** transforms categorical bins into a continuous numeric score; **IV** aggregates WoE to rank feature importance. - **IV thresholds:** $< 0.02$ useless, $0.02$–$0.1$ weak, $0.1$–$0.3$ medium, $0.3$–$0.5$ strong, $> 0.5$ suspicious. - **Gini** $= 2 \times AUC - 1$; aim for $Gini \geq 0.4$ in production scorecards. - **KS statistic** measures maximum separation between cumulative good and bad distributions; aim for $KS \geq 0.3$. - **PSI** detects population drift; alert threshold is **0.25**. - **Big data pipeline:** Ingestion (Kafka/Flume) → Storage (HDFS/HBase) → Processing (Spark) → Modeling (MLlib/H2O) → Scoring (real-time API) → Monitoring (PSI/AUC drift). - **Alternative data** (mobile, social, telco) enables scoring of **thin-file** customers excluded by traditional bureaus. - **Risk-adjusted pricing formula:** $r_{\min} = \text{PD} \times \text{LGD} + \text{Operating Cost} + \text{Profit Margin}$. - **Model interpretability is a regulatory requirement**, not optional — explainable AI (XAI) techniques like SHAP/LIME are increasingly expected by RBI and Basel auditors. - **Graph features** (PageRank, community detection) are powerful for detecting **fraud rings** and **connected defaults**. ---

topics.title: Business Process Analytics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT864 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Business Process Analytics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Business Process Analytics (BPA)** is the discipline of systematically capturing, reconstructing, analysing, and improving **operational business processes** by leveraging event data recorded in modern information systems. It is the *analytical counterpart* of **Business Process Management (BPM)** and is most rigorously implemented through **Process Mining** — a family of techniques that sit at the intersection of *Data Mining*, *Business Process Modelling*, and *Process-aware Information Systems*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Examination-Verbatim):**
> *Business Process Analytics is the practice of deriving actionable, evidence-based insights about the execution, performance, and conformance of business processes from event logs generated by enterprise information systems, using process discovery, conformance checking, and enhancement techniques.*

The three primary pillars of Business Process Analytics (aligned with the **Process Mining Manifesto**, IEEE Task Force on Process Mining) are:

1. **Process Discovery** — constructing a process model (e.g., a Petri net, BPMN diagram, or directly-follows graph) from raw event logs *without* prior process knowledge.
2. **Conformance Checking** — comparing an *a priori* (existing) process model against the *as-is* event log to detect deviations, bottlenecks, and compliance violations.
3. **Enhancement** — extending or improving an existing process model using the event log data (e.g., annotating models with performance metrics, repairing models).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Hospital Trail" Analogy:**
> Imagine a large hospital where every patient carries a **smart wristband**. The moment they enter the reception, get a blood test, consult a doctor, undergo an MRI, and finally pay the bill — the wristband **records an event** with a timestamp, activity, and resource (nurse/doctor/machine).

| Real-World Hospital | Business Process Analytics |
|---|---|
| Each patient wristband record | **Event** in the event log |
| Patient ID | **Case ID** |
| "Blood test", "Doctor consult" | **Activity** (task) |
| Timestamp of each step | **Timestamp** |
| Nurse / Doctor / MRI machine | **Resource** |
| Total time patient spent in hospital | **Cycle Time** |
| Wasted waiting between tests | **Waiting Time** |
| Discovering the real patient journey | **Process Discovery** |
| Checking if insurance protocols were followed | **Conformance Checking** |
| Redesigning the patient flow to cut wait time | **Enhancement** |

In this analogy, **Business Process Analytics** is the hospital administrator's "data-driven microscope" — it uses the *digital breadcrumbs* (event logs) left by every process execution to reconstruct the *true* process, measure its efficiency, and prescribe improvements — **without interviewing a single patient**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in Business Process Analytics

The following metrics are standardised by the IEEE Task Force on Process Mining:

- **Cycle Time** — total end-to-end time for a case, denoted $C_i$ for case $i$.
- **Service (Processing) Time** — time the resource is *actively* working on the case.
- **Waiting (Idle) Time** — time the case is queued, waiting for a resource.
- **Throughput Time** — synonymous with cycle time in most KTU texts.
- **Throughput** — number of cases completed per unit time, typically $\mathbf{cases/hour}$.
- **Resource Utilization** — fraction of available time a resource is busy.
- **MTTR / MTBF** — Mean Time To Repair / Mean Time Between Failures (operational KPI).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **business process** is *not* the same as a *business function*. A process is an **ordered chain of activities** that transforms an input (a customer order) into an output (a delivered product), executed across one or more organisational units.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Business Process Analytics Pipeline (End-to-End Logic)

The analytical flow, decomposed into six auditable steps, is:

1. **Data Extraction (ELT/ETL)** — extract event logs from heterogeneous sources: ERP (SAP, Oracle), CRM (Salesforce), BPM engines (Camunda, IBM BPM), ticketing systems (ServiceNow, JIRA), and IoT sensor streams.
2. **Event Log Construction** — format each row as a tuple $(case\_id, activity, timestamp, resource, \dots)$ ensuring a **single Case notion**, a **single Activity notion**, and a **monotonically ordered Timestamp** per case.
3. **Process Discovery** — apply an algorithm (e.g., $\alpha$-algorithm, Heuristic Miner, Inductive Miner, Fuzzy Miner) to derive a process model from the log.
4. **Conformance Checking** — replay the log on the discovered/reference model; compute **fitness, precision, generalisation, simplicity** (the four quality dimensions of process models).
5. **Performance / Bottleneck Analysis** — overlay timing information onto the model; identify the activity with the highest *average waiting time* or *highest utilisation* — the **bottleneck**.
6. **Predictive / Prescriptive Analytics** — train ML models (LSTM, XGBoost) on historical traces to predict *remaining time*, *next activity*, or *outcome* (e.g., will this loan application be approved?).

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

| Step | Why it matters in Engineering Practice |
|---|---|
| **Log construction** | Real-world logs are *dirty*: out-of-order timestamps, missing resources, multi-instance activities. Without normalisation, every downstream metric is wrong. |
| **Discovery** | Stakeholders' mental models of the process are *almost always wrong* — analytics reveals the *de facto* process. |
| **Conformance** | Regulatory compliance (GDPR, SOX, HIPAA) requires proof that the *de jure* (documented) process matches reality. |
| **Performance analysis** | Identifies the *single activity* responsible for 80% of delay (Pareto principle applied to waiting time). |
| **Predictive analytics** | Enables **prescriptive routing** — dynamic case assignment to avoid predicted bottlenecks. |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains every formula a KTU student must memorise for Module 2 — Business Process Analytics.** All symbols use $\vert$ (rendered as `\vert`) to avoid breaking the markdown table.

| # | Metric / Concept | Formula (KTU Exam-Ready) | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cycle Time** of case $i$ | $C_i = t_{end}^{(i)} - t_{start}^{(i)}$ | minutes / hours | First–to-last event of a case |
| 2 | **Average Cycle Time** | $\bar{C} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} C_i$ | minutes | Used for SLA benchmarking |
| 3 | **Service Time** of activity $a$ | $S_a = \dfrac{1}{n_a} \sum_{j=1}^{n_a} (c_j^{(a)} - s_j^{(a)})$ | minutes | $n_a$ = executions of $a$ |
| 4 | **Waiting Time** of case $i$ at activity $a$ | $W_i^{(a)} = s_i^{(a)} - c_i^{(prev)}$ | minutes | $prev$ = preceding activity |
| 5 | **Total Waiting Time** (case $i$) | $W_i = \sum_{a \in A} W_i^{(a)}$ | minutes | Excludes actual processing |
| 6 | **Total Service Time** (case $i$) | $T_i = \sum_{a \in A} S_i^{(a)}$ | minutes | Sum of all processing slots |
| 7 | **Throughput** | $\Theta = \dfrac{N}{t_{end} - t_{start}}$ | cases / hour | N cases finished in window |
| 8 | **Resource Utilization** (resource $r$) | $U_r = \dfrac{\text{BusyTime}_r}{\text{AvailableTime}_r} \times 100\%$ | % | $\vert U_r \vert \leq 1$ typically |
| 9 | **Bottleneck Criterion** | $a^{*} = \arg\max_{a \in A} S_a$ | — | Highest avg service time |
| 10 | **Conformance Fitness** | $f = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(1 - \dfrac{m}{p}\right) + \dfrac{1}{2}\left(1 - \dfrac{r}{m}\right)$ | $\vert f \vert \in [0,1]$ | $p$=produced, $m$=consumed, $r$=remaining |
| 11 | **Precision** | $\text{prec} = 1 - \dfrac{\sum_{a \in A} \vert \text{escaping edges}_a \vert}{\text{total escaping edges}}$ | [0,1] | Higher = less under-fitting |
| 12 | **Generalisation** | $g$ from alignment log-likelihood | [0,1] | Penalises over-fitting |
| 13 | **Simplicity** | $\text{simp} = 1 - \dfrac{\vert X \vert}{\vert \text{max nodes} \vert}$ | [0,1] | Occam's razor |
| 14 | **Little's Law (queueing)** | $L = \lambda \cdot W$ | — | $L$=avg in-system, $\lambda$=arrival rate, $W$=avg time |
| 15 | **Process Variant Count** | $V = \vert \{\text{unique traces in log}\} \vert$ | integer | Used in variant analysis |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Banking & Finance** — loan approval process analytics reduce average sanction time from 14 days to 3 days by identifying manual underwriting as the bottleneck.
- **Healthcare** — Erasmus MC (Rotterdam) used process mining to **reduce ICU waiting time by 30%** by visualising patient flows.
- **Manufacturing** — Toyota's *kaizen* is now augmented with conformance mining to detect deviations from the *jikoda* (autonomation) standard.
- **IT Service Management** — ING Bank used process mining to consolidate 432 process variants into 19 standardised end-to-end processes, saving **€50 million/year**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Every modern Distributed System (microservices, event-driven architectures) is a *business process* in disguise. The same BPA techniques apply to **Kafka event streams**, **REST API call chains**, and **CI/CD pipelines** — making this topic foundational for *DevOps analytics* and *AIOps*.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked-Out Numerical Derivation: From Event Log to Bottleneck Identification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** The KTU board typically provides a 4–6 row event log and asks the student to (i) build a process model, (ii) compute cycle / waiting / service times, (iii) identify the bottleneck. The following exhaustive derivation models the **exact valuation pattern** expected.

#### 3.1.1 Given Event Log (synthetic, KTU-style)

A loan-approval process is recorded in the table below. Each row is an event; $c_i^{(a)}$ denotes *completion* time of activity $a$ for case $i$.

| Case ID | Activity | Start Time | End Time | Resource |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| C1 | Receive Application | 09:00 | 09:20 | Clerk-A |
| C1 | Credit Check | 09:20 | 09:35 | Officer-B |
| C1 | Manager Approval | 09:35 | 10:10 | Mgr-C |
| C2 | Receive Application | 09:10 | 09:30 | Clerk-A |
| C2 | Credit Check | 09:30 | 09:40 | Officer-B |
| C2 | Manager Approval | 09:40 | 09:55 | Mgr-C |
| C3 | Receive Application | 09:25 | 09:45 | Clerk-A |
| C3 | Credit Check | 09:45 | 10:20 | Officer-B |
| C3 | Manager Approval | 10:20 | 10:40 | Mgr-C |

#### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Compute Service Time per Activity per Case

The **service time** of activity $a$ for case $i$ is simply the difference between its *end* and *start* timestamps:

$$
S_i^{(a)} = c_i^{(a)} - s_i^{(a)}
$$

For **Activity A — Receive Application** (3 cases):

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{C1}^{(A)} &= 09{:}20 - 09{:}00 = 20 \text{ min} \\
S_{C2}^{(A)} &= 09{:}30 - 09{:}10 = 20 \text{ min} \\
S_{C3}^{(A)} &= 09{:}45 - 09{:}25 = 20 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

For **Activity B — Credit Check** (3 cases):

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{C1}^{(B)} &= 09{:}35 - 09{:}20 = 15 \text{ min} \\
S_{C2}^{(B)} &= 09{:}40 - 09{:}30 = 10 \text{ min} \\
S_{C3}^{(B)} &= 10{:}20 - 09{:}45 = 35 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

For **Activity C — Manager Approval** (3 cases):

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{C1}^{(C)} &= 10{:}10 - 09{:}35 = 35 \text{ min} \\
S_{C2}^{(C)} &= 09{:}55 - 09{:}40 = 15 \text{ min} \\
S_{C3}^{(C)} &= 10{:}40 - 10{:}20 = 20 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** Each event carries an *implicit* start timestamp derived from the *previous* event's end timestamp for that case, because the log records only the completion instant. The first event's start is read directly; for all subsequent events of the same case, $s_i^{(a)} = c_i^{(prev)}$.

#### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Compute Average Service Time per Activity

Applying the standard average formula

$$
\bar{S}_a = \frac{1}{n_a} \sum_{j=1}^{n_a} S_j^{(a)}
$$

we obtain:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{S}_{A} &= \frac{20 + 20 + 20}{3} = \frac{60}{3} = 20 \text{ min} \\
\bar{S}_{B} &= \frac{15 + 10 + 35}{3} = \frac{60}{3} = 20 \text{ min} \\
\bar{S}_{C} &= \frac{35 + 15 + 20}{3} = \frac{70}{3} \approx 23.33 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Compute Waiting Time per Activity per Case

The **waiting time** for activity $a$ in case $i$ is the gap between the *start* of $a$ and the *end* of the *previous* activity of the same case:

$$
W_i^{(a)} = s_i^{(a)} - c_i^{(prev)}
$$

The first activity of a case has **zero waiting time** by convention (no predecessor).

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{C1}^{(A)} &= 0, \quad W_{C2}^{(A)} = 0, \quad W_{C3}^{(A)} = 0 \\
W_{C1}^{(B)} &= 09{:}20 - 09{:}20 = 0 \text{ min} \\
W_{C2}^{(B)} &= 09{:}30 - 09{:}30 = 0 \text{ min} \\
W_{C3}^{(B)} &= 09{:}45 - 09{:}45 = 0 \text{ min} \\
W_{C1}^{(C)} &= 09{:}35 - 09{:}35 = 0 \text{ min} \\
W_{C2}^{(C)} &= 09{:}40 - 09{:}40 = 0 \text{ min} \\
W_{C3}^{(C)} &= 10{:}20 - 10{:}20 = 0 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** The fact that all waiting times are zero in this clean log indicates an **unconstrained resource model** — there is no queueing. In a real log, you would almost always observe positive waiting times, and the activity with the highest *average* waiting time is typically the **structural bottleneck**.

#### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Compute Cycle Time per Case

$$
C_i = t_{end}^{(i)} - t_{start}^{(i)}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{C1} &= 10{:}10 - 09{:}00 = 70 \text{ min} \\
C_{C2} &= 09{:}55 - 09{:}10 = 45 \text{ min} \\
C_{C3} &= 10{:}40 - 09{:}25 = 75 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### 3.1.6 Step 5 — Compute Average Cycle Time

$$
\bar{C} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} C_i = \frac{70 + 45 + 75}{3} = \frac{190}{3} \approx 63.33 \text{ min}
$$

#### 3.1.7 Step 6 — Compute Throughput

The observation window spans from the *earliest* start (09:00) to the *latest* end (10:40), i.e., 100 minutes:

$$
\Theta = \frac{N}{t_{end} - t_{start}} = \frac{3 \text{ cases}}{100 \text{ min}} = 0.03 \text{ cases/min} = 1.8 \text{ cases/hour}
$$

#### 3.1.8 Step 7 — Compute Resource Utilisation

For each resource, **Busy Time** is the sum of service times across all cases that resource processed; **Available Time** is the window duration (100 min). 

$$
U_r = \frac{\text{BusyTime}_r}{\text{AvailableTime}_r} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{Clerk-A} &= \frac{20 + 20 + 20}{100} = 60\% \\
U_{Officer-B} &= \frac{15 + 10 + 35}{100} = 60\% \\
U_{Mgr-C} &= \frac{35 + 15 + 20}{100} = 70\%
\end{aligned}
$$

#### 3.1.9 Step 8 — Identify the Bottleneck

By the **Bottleneck Criterion** from the formula sheet:

$$
a^{*} = \arg\max_{a \in A} \bar{S}_a
$$

We have $\bar{S}_{A} = 20$, $\bar{S}_{B} = 20$, $\bar{S}_{C} \approx 23.33$ — so

$$
a^{*} = \text{Manager Approval (Activity C)}
$$

This is corroborated by **Mgr-C** having the **highest resource utilisation (70%)** — both metrics converge on the same conclusion.

### 3.2 Summary Matrix of Computed Metrics

| Metric | Value | Bottleneck? |
|---|---|---|
| $\bar{S}_{A}$ | 20.00 min | No |
| $\bar{S}_{B}$ | 20.00 min | No |
| $\bar{S}_{C}$ | 23.33 min | **Yes** |
| $\bar{C}$ | 63.33 min | — |
| $\Theta$ | 1.8 cases/hr | — |
| $U_{Clerk-A}$ | 60% | — |
| $U_{Officer-B}$ | 60% | — |
| $U_{Mgr-C}$ | 70% | **Yes** |

### 3.3 Verification via Little's Law (Sanity Check)

$$
L = \lambda \cdot W
$$

With average arrival rate $\lambda = 3/100 = 0.03$ cases/min and average in-system time $W = \bar{C} = 63.33$ min:

$$
L = 0.03 \times 63.33 = 1.9 \approx 2 \text{ cases in system on average}
$$

This is consistent with the observation that **2–3 cases are in-flight** at any time (C1, C2, C3 overlapping in the window). The derivation is internally consistent.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Business Process Analytics Pipeline (Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **complete analytical pipeline** for a BPA system, from raw event ingestion to prescriptive recommendation. Each stage is annotated with the KTU exam-relevant technique used.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    src1[Source A\nERP Logs]
    src2[Source B\nCRM Systems]
    src3[Source C\nIoT Sensors]
    src4[Source D\nBPM Engine]

    ext[ETL Extractor\nApache Nifi / Kafka]
    clean[Event Log Cleaner\nFiltering, Sorting, Imputation]
    xes[XES Event Log\nStandard IEEE 1849-2016]

    disc1[Alpha Algorithm]
    disc2[Heuristic Miner]
    disc3[Inductive Miner]
    disc[Process Discovery Module\nSelected Algorithm]

    pm[Process Model\nPetri Net / BPMN]
    cf[Conformance Checker\nToken Replay / Alignments]

    fit[Fitness Score\n0 to 1]
    prec[Precision Score\n0 to 1]
    perf[Performance Analysis\nBottleneck Heatmap]

    ml1[ML Predictor\nRemaining Time LSTM]
    ml2[ML Predictor\nNext Activity XGBoost]
    ml3[ML Predictor\nOutcome Classifier]

    pres[Prescriptive Engine\nDynamic Routing]
    dash[Executive Dashboard\nCelonis / Disco / ProM]

    src1 --> ext
    src2 --> ext
    src3 --> ext
    src4 --> ext
    ext --> clean
    clean --> xes
    xes --> disc1
    xes --> disc2
    xes --> disc3
    disc1 --> disc
    disc2 --> disc
    disc3 --> disc
    disc --> pm
    pm --> cf
    xes --> cf
    cf --> fit
    cf --> prec
    xes --> perf
    pm --> perf
    xes --> ml1
    xes --> ml2
    xes --> ml3
    ml1 --> pres
    ml2 --> pres
    ml3 --> pres
    fit --> dash
    prec --> dash
    perf --> dash
    pres --> dash
```

### 4.2 Discovered Process Model for the Loan-Approval Worked Example

The following Mermaid block renders the **Petri-Net-equivalent directly-follows graph (DFG)** discovered from the event log in Section 3.1.1.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    start[Start Event] --> actA[Receive Application\nService 20 min]
    actA --> actB[Credit Check\nService 20 min]
    actB --> actC[Manager Approval\nService 23.33 min]
    actC --> endE[End Event]

    style actC fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#c0392b,color:#ffffff
    style actA fill:#74b9ff,stroke:#0984e3,color:#ffffff
    style actB fill:#74b9ff,stroke:#0984e3,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Activity C is highlighted in red because both its average service time and its resource utilisation are the highest in the system. In a real Celonis / Disco dashboard, the *edge thickness* would represent frequency and the *node colour intensity* would represent time — both converging on **Manager Approval** as the bottleneck.

### 4.3 Conformance Checking — Token Replay Visual

The following diagram shows the conceptual difference between **fitness = 1** (perfect log-model fit) and **fitness < 1** (deviation detected).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Event Log Trace] --> B{Token Replay Engine}
    B --> C[Compare Against Model]
    C --> D{Move Synchronisation}
    D --> E[Synchronous Move\nLog and Model Agree]
    D --> F[Log Move\nOnly in Log]
    D --> G[Model Move\nOnly in Model]
    E --> H[Fitness Computation]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Fitness Score 0 to 1]
    I --> J{Decision}
    J --> K[Fitness = 1\nPerfect Conformance]
    J --> L[Fitness less than 0.8\nDeviation Flagged]
    L --> M[Root Cause Analysis]
    M --> N[Process Improvement Action]
```

### 4.4 Lifecycle Diagram — Continuous Process Improvement Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    p1[Phase 1 Process Identification\nDefine Scope and Goals]
    p2[Phase 2 Discovery\nMine the As Is Process]
    p3[Phase 3 Analysis\nBottleneck and Variant Analysis]
    p4[Phase 4 Redesign\nTo Be Process Model]
    p5[Phase 5 Implementation\nDeploy Improved Process]
    p6[Phase 6 Monitoring\nContinuous Conformance Check]
    p1 --> p2
    p2 --> p3
    p3 --> p4
    p4 --> p5
    p5 --> p6
    p6 --> p3

    style p3 fill:#ffeaa7,stroke:#fdcb6e
    style p6 fill:#55efc4,stroke:#00b894
```

> [!NOTE]
> **The Feedback Arrow from Phase 6 back to Phase 3** represents the *closed-loop* nature of BPA — the monitored event log of the *new* process is continuously re-mined to detect *regression* or *fresh* bottlenecks. This is the **BPM lifecycle** that KTU examiners frequently test in 14-mark questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
*Define Business Process Analytics. List its three primary pillars as identified by the IEEE Task Force on Process Mining.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — Examiner-Verbatim Style):**

> **Business Process Analytics (BPA)** is the discipline of analysing event logs recorded by enterprise information systems to derive insights into the *execution, performance, and conformance* of operational business processes.
>
> The three primary pillars, as standardised by the IEEE Task Force on Process Mining (2011), are:
>
> **(i) Process Discovery** — extracting a process model directly from event logs without prior knowledge.
> **(ii) Conformance Checking** — comparing an *a priori* model with the event log to detect deviations, fraud, or non-compliance.
> **(iii) Enhancement** — extending or repairing an existing model using event log data, e.g., adding performance annotations.
>
> *(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for the three pillars — ½ mark each with one-line description.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*What is an XES event log? Mention any four mandatory attributes of an event record as per the IEEE 1849-2016 XES standard.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **XES (eXtensible Event Stream)** is the IEEE 1849-2016 standard XML-based format used to store event logs for process mining. It is the *de facto* interchange format between tools like ProM, Celonis, Disco, and Minit.
>
> The four mandatory attributes of an event record are:
>
> 1. **Case ID** (`concept:name` of the trace) — uniquely identifies one process instance.
> 2. **Activity Name** (`concept:name` of the event) — the task performed.
> 3. **Timestamp** (`time:timestamp`) — when the event occurred.
> 4. **Resource / Agent** (`org:resource`) — the human or system executing the event.
>
> *(1 mark for XES definition + 2 marks for the four attributes — ½ mark each.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **three types of process mining** with suitable real-world examples. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Consider the following event log of a hospital's *Patient Discharge* process. Compute the **average service time** for each activity, the **average cycle time**, and **identify the bottleneck**.

| Case ID | Activity | Start | End | Resource |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| P01 | Registration | 08:00 | 08:10 | R1 |
| P01 | Doctor Consult | 08:10 | 08:55 | D1 |
| P01 | Pharmacy | 08:55 | 09:10 | P1 |
| P02 | Registration | 08:15 | 08:25 | R1 |
| P02 | Doctor Consult | 08:25 | 08:50 | D1 |
| P02 | Pharmacy | 08:50 | 09:00 | P1 |
| P03 | Registration | 08:30 | 08:40 | R1 |
| P03 | Doctor Consult | 08:40 | 09:30 | D1 |
| P03 | Pharmacy | 09:30 | 09:45 | P1 |

*(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Three Types of Process Mining (7 marks)**

| Sub-type | Full Description | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Process Discovery (3 marks)** | *Process Discovery* builds a process model *purely* from the event log, without using any *a priori* model. Algorithms include the $\alpha$-algorithm, Heuristic Miner, Inductive Miner, and Fuzzy Miner. The output is typically a Petri net, BPMN model, or Directly-Follows Graph. | *Example:* Mining the AS-IS patient flow in a hospital from 1 million discharge records to visualise the *actual* discharge process, revealing that 12% of cases skip the Pharmacy step. |
| **2. Conformance Checking (2 marks)** | *Conformance Checking* compares an *a priori* model with the *de facto* event log using **token replay** or **alignment-based** techniques. It quantifies four quality dimensions: **fitness** (does the log fit the model?), **precision** (does the model avoid behaviours not in the log?), **generalisation** (does the model avoid over-fitting?), and **simplicity** (Occam's razor). | *Example:* A bank's documented loan approval process requires 4 mandatory steps, but conformance checking on the log reveals that 23% of approved loans skipped the credit check — flagging a regulatory SOX violation. |
| **3. Enhancement (2 marks)** | *Enhancement* uses the event log to *improve* or *extend* an existing model — for example, by adding performance data (waiting time, throughput), repairing missing transitions, or projecting a *to-be* model. | *Example:* A logistics company overlays average delivery times onto each arc of its shipment model to identify that the "Customs Clearance" arc consistently adds 48 hours, prompting renegotiation with the customs broker. |

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Service Times per Activity per Case (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{P01}^{(Reg)} &= 08{:}10 - 08{:}00 = 10 \text{ min} \\
S_{P02}^{(Reg)} &= 08{:}25 - 08{:}15 = 10 \text{ min} \\
S_{P03}^{(Reg)} &= 08{:}40 - 08{:}30 = 10 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{P01}^{(Doc)} &= 08{:}55 - 08{:}10 = 45 \text{ min} \\
S_{P02}^{(Doc)} &= 08{:}50 - 08{:}25 = 25 \text{ min} \\
S_{P03}^{(Doc)} &= 09{:}30 - 08{:}40 = 50 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{P01}^{(Phar)} &= 09{:}10 - 08{:}55 = 15 \text{ min} \\
S_{P02}^{(Phar)} &= 09{:}00 - 08{:}50 = 10 \text{ min} \\
S_{P03}^{(Phar)} &= 09{:}45 - 09{:}30 = 15 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Average Service Time per Activity (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{S}_{Reg} &= \frac{10 + 10 + 10}{3} = 10.00 \text{ min} \\
\bar{S}_{Doc} &= \frac{45 + 25 + 50}{3} = 40.00 \text{ min} \\
\bar{S}_{Phar} &= \frac{15 + 10 + 15}{3} = \frac{40}{3} \approx 13.33 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Cycle Times per Case (1.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{P01} &= 09{:}10 - 08{:}00 = 70 \text{ min} \\
C_{P02} &= 09{:}00 - 08{:}15 = 45 \text{ min} \\
C_{P03} &= 09{:}45 - 08{:}30 = 75 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Average Cycle Time (0.5 mark):**

$$
\bar{C} = \frac{70 + 45 + 75}{3} = \frac{190}{3} \approx 63.33 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 5 — Bottleneck Identification (1 mark):**

$$
a^{*} = \arg\max\{10.00,\ 40.00,\ 13.33\} = \textbf{Doctor Consult}
$$

with $\bar{S}_{Doc} = 40$ min — **the highest average service time**, accounting for 63% of total processing time. The hospital should consider adding a second doctor or triaging cases before consultation.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Discuss the **Business Process Management (BPM) lifecycle** in detail, mapping each phase to its corresponding *process mining technique*. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** What is **conformance checking**? With the help of a small example, explain the **token replay** algorithm and the computation of the **fitness metric**. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) BPM Lifecycle Mapped to Process Mining (7 marks — 1 mark per phase + 0.5 for technique):**

| Phase | Description | Process-Mining Linkage |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Process Identification** | Define the *boundaries* of the process to be analysed — which cases, which activities, which time window, which organisational unit. | **Log Filtering** — pre-process the XES log to retain only the in-scope traces. |
| **2. Process Discovery (As-Is)** | Reconstruct the *actual* process from the event log without any prior model. | **$\alpha$-Algorithm, Heuristic Miner, Inductive Miner** — return a Petri net / BPMN model. |
| **3. Process Analysis** | Diagnose bottlenecks, variants, deviations, and performance issues. | **Performance Analysis, Variant Analysis, Social Network Analysis, Decision Mining.** |
| **4. Process Redesign (To-Be)** | Design the *target* process to fix identified issues. | **Enhancement** — annotate the discovered model with proposed improvements; simulate the *to-be* model. |
| **5. Process Implementation** | Deploy the redesigned process into the BPM engine (Camunda, IBM BPM, Appian). | **Continuous Discovery** — start logging the *new* process from day one. |
| **6. Process Monitoring & Control** | Continuously compare the running process against the *to-be* model. | **Conformance Checking (Token Replay, Alignments)** — flag drift, regression, or compliance violations in real time. |

The lifecycle is **iterative** — step 6 feeds back to step 3 (re-analysis) to enable **continuous improvement**, formalised in frameworks like BPM CBOK and supported by the **Deming PDCA cycle**.

**(b) Conformance Checking, Token Replay & Fitness (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** *Conformance Checking* is the process-mining technique that quantifies the *degree of agreement* between an *a priori* process model and an observed event log. It produces four metrics: **fitness, precision, generalisation, simplicity**.

**Token Replay Algorithm (3 marks — worked with an example):**

Consider the following trivial model: **A → B → C** (3 sequential activities).

Suppose the log contains the trace: **A, B, B, C** (an *extra* execution of B).

**Step-by-step Token Replay:**

| Step | Event in Log | Model State (Tokens) | Move Type | Result |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | A | Initial: 0 tokens at start | Produce token on start | OK |
| 2 | A | Consume A's input, produce A's output | Log-Model sync | OK |
| 3 | B | Consume B's input, produce B's output | Log-Model sync | OK |
| 4 | B | No token left for B (already consumed) | **Log Move** (B in log, no model token) | **Missing token** |
| 5 | C | Token present from previous B | Log-Model sync | OK |
| 6 | End | Consume C's output, produce on end | Log-Model sync | OK |

- **p** = number of tokens *produced* during replay = 3 (start, A-output, B-output) + 1 (B extra attempt) = **4**
- **c** = number of tokens *consumed* = 3 (A, B, C) + 1 (missing for extra B) = **3 successful + 1 missing**
- **m** = number of *missing* tokens (events that could not be replayed) = **1** (the second B)
- **r** = number of *remaining* tokens at the end (unconsumed) = **0**

**Fitness Computation (2 marks):**

$$
f = \frac{1}{2}\left(1 - \frac{m}{p}\right) + \frac{1}{2}\left(1 - \frac{r}{c}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\left(1 - \frac{1}{4}\right) + \frac{1}{2}\left(1 - \frac{0}{3}\right) = \frac{1}{2}(0.75) + \frac{1}{2}(1.00) = 0.875
$$

A fitness of 0.875 indicates **high but imperfect** conformance — the model fits the log 87.5% of the time. In KTU exams, $f \geq 0.8$ is conventionally considered *acceptable conformance*.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "service time" with "waiting time"** — Service time is *active* processing ($c_i - s_i$ for the same activity); waiting time is *idle* time between activities ($s_{next} - c_{prev}$). Examiners allocate **1 mark** specifically for this distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting the "case notion"** — Every event log *must* have a clearly defined Case ID. If a student fails to identify it, **2 marks are forfeited** in any process-discovery question.
> 3. **Incorrect bottleneck criterion** — A common error is to mark the activity with the *highest cycle time* as the bottleneck. The **correct** criterion is the activity with the *highest average service time* ($a^* = \arg\max_a \bar{S}_a$) **or** highest *resource utilisation* ($U_r$). Cycle time is a *case-level* metric, not an *activity-level* one.
> 4. **Mixing up conformance metrics** — *Fitness* measures whether the log *fits* the model; *precision* measures whether the model is *not too general*; *generalisation* measures whether the model is *not too specific*; *simplicity* applies Occam's razor. Examiners deduct marks for conflating these.
> 5. **Skipping units** — A naked number "20" is worth less than "20 min" or "20 seconds". Always append the unit.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 2 — Business Process Analytics: Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Definition** — BPA is the *data-driven* reconstruction, analysis, and improvement of business processes using event logs. The three pillars are **Discovery, Conformance, Enhancement**.
- **Event Log Format** — XES (IEEE 1849-2016) is the standard. Mandatory attributes: **Case ID, Activity, Timestamp, Resource**.
- **Key Metrics (Must Memorise):**
  - Cycle Time $C_i = t_{end} - t_{start}$ (per case)
  - Average Service Time $\bar{S}_a = \frac{1}{n_a}\sum S_j^{(a)}$ (per activity)
  - Waiting Time $W_i^{(a)} = s_i^{(a)} - c_i^{(prev)}$
  - Throughput $\Theta = N / (t_{end} - t_{start})$
  - Resource Utilisation $U_r = \text{BusyTime} / \text{AvailableTime}$
  - Little's Law $L = \lambda \cdot W$ — *always* useful as a sanity check.
- **Bottleneck Criterion** — $a^{*} = \arg\max_a \bar{S}_a$ **OR** $\arg\max_r U_r$ (the two must agree).
- **Process Mining Algorithms** — $\alpha$-algorithm (classical, fails on noise), Heuristic Miner (noise-robust), Inductive Miner (guarantees sound models), Fuzzy Miner (large/spaghetti logs).
- **Conformance Checking** — Token Replay produces four quality dimensions: **Fitness, Precision, Generalisation, Simplicity**. Fitness of 1.0 = perfect fit.
- **BPM Lifecycle** — Six iterative phases: Identification → Discovery → Analysis → Redesign → Implementation → Monitoring. The feedback loop from Monitoring back to Analysis enables *continuous improvement*.
- **Tools** — *ProM* (open-source, academic), *Celonis* (industry leader, paid), *Disco* (Fluxicon, paid, user-friendly), *Minit* (Microsoft acquired).
- **Real-World Wins** — ING Bank saved €50 M/year; Siemens reduced order-to-cash cycle by 50%; Erasmus MC reduced ICU wait by 30%.
- **Big-Data Linkage** — Modern BPA operates on *distributed* event stores (Kafka, Pulsar, Event Hubs) using *parallel* mining algorithms (e.g., **distributed Inductive Miner** in Apache Flink).
- **Examiner Heuristics** — Always define your Case ID, always include units, always label the bottleneck criterion, always show a process diagram for any discovery question worth ≥ 7 marks.
- **Common Confusions to Avoid:**
  - BPM ≠ BPA (BPM is *managing* processes; BPA is *analysing* them).
  - Process *discovery* ≠ process *modelling* (discovery is data-driven; modelling is human-driven).
  - Fitness ≠ Precision (fitness is recall; precision is the inverse of over-generalisation).
  - Throughput *time* ≠ Throughput *rate* (time is per-case in minutes; rate is cases-per-hour).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** For any 14-mark BPA question, follow the **4-Part Answer Template**:
> (1) Define key terms (1 mark),
> (2) Show the process model / diagram (3 marks),
> (3) Compute the requested metrics step-by-step (6 marks),
> (4) Provide interpretation / bottleneck identification (4 marks).
> This template aligns exactly with KTU's outcome-based marking scheme and maximises your score.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Predictive and Descriptive Analytics - Supervised Learning - Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT864 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Predictive and Descriptive Analytics - Supervised Learning - Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Two Pillars of Analytics: Predictive vs. Descriptive

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Analytics is broadly classified into **Descriptive Analytics** (answers "What happened?") and **Predictive Analytics** (answers "What will happen?"). Under Predictive Analytics, **Supervised Learning** is the most heavily tested paradigm in KTU examinations.

### Formal Definition

**Descriptive Analytics** is the branch of data analytics that uses historical data, statistical techniques, and data visualization to summarize, interpret, and report patterns and trends. It focuses on understanding past behavior. Common techniques include clustering, association rule mining, and data aggregation.

**Predictive Analytics** is the branch of data analytics that employs statistical models, machine learning algorithms, and historical data to forecast future outcomes, behaviors, or trends. It is forward-looking and probabilistic in nature. Common techniques include classification, regression, and time-series forecasting.

### Conceptual Analogy: The Weather Reporter

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> - **Descriptive Analytics** is like reading a **weather report of yesterday**. The reporter tells you: "Yesterday, it rained 3 inches, and the temperature was 28°C." They are simply summarizing past events.
> - **Predictive Analytics** is like the **weather forecast for tomorrow**. The reporter uses historical patterns, satellite data, and mathematical models to say: "There is an 80% chance of rain tomorrow."
> - **Supervised Learning (Classification)** is like teaching a child to identify fruits by showing them labeled examples: "This is an Apple, this is a Banana, this is a Mango." After seeing enough labeled fruits, the child can correctly identify a new, unseen fruit.

## 1.2 Supervised Learning: The Labeled-Data Paradigm

**Supervised Learning** is a machine learning paradigm in which an algorithm learns a mapping function $f: X \rightarrow Y$ from an input feature space $X$ to an output label space $Y$, using a training dataset $\{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{n}$ where each input $x_i$ is paired with a known, human-annotated target label $y_i$.

The "supervisor" or "teacher" provides the correct answers (labels) during training, and the model generalizes from these examples to make predictions on new, unseen data.

### Key Terminology (Bolded KTU Standards)

- **Feature Vector ($\mathbf{x}$):** An n-dimensional input vector representing the attributes of a data point.
- **Label ($y$):** The known output or class associated with a data point.
- **Training Set:** The labeled dataset used to train the model.
- **Test Set:** The unseen labeled dataset used to evaluate the model.
- **Hypothesis Function ($h_\theta(\mathbf{x})$):** The learned function that maps inputs to predicted outputs.
- **Loss Function ($J(\theta)$):** A measure of the error between predicted and actual labels.

## 1.3 Classification: The Supervised Sub-Discipline

**Classification** is a supervised learning task where the target variable $y$ is a **discrete, categorical** label drawn from a finite set of classes $C = \{c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_k\}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Distinction for KTU Exams:**
> - If $y$ is **categorical** (e.g., Spam/Not Spam, Disease/No Disease) → **Classification**
> - If $y$ is **continuous** (e.g., House Price, Temperature) → **Regression**

### Types of Classification

| Type | Description | Example |
|------|-------------|---------|
| **Binary Classification** | Exactly 2 mutually exclusive classes | Email: Spam vs. Ham |
| **Multiclass Classification** | More than 2 exclusive classes | Digit recognition: 0-9 |
| **Multi-label Classification** | Multiple non-exclusive labels per instance | Movie genres: Action, Comedy, Drama |

### Major Classification Algorithms in KTU Syllabus

1. **Decision Trees (ID3, C4.5, CART)**
2. **k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN)**
3. **Naive Bayes Classifier**
4. **Support Vector Machines (SVM)**
5. **Logistic Regression**
6. **Random Forest & Ensemble Methods**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Supervised Learning Workflow

The operational pipeline of any supervised classification task follows these structured steps:

1. **Data Collection:** Gather a labeled dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$.
2. **Data Preprocessing:** Handle missing values, normalize features, encode categorical variables, and split data.
3. **Train-Test Split:** Partition $\mathcal{D}$ into $\mathcal{D}_{train}$ (typically 70-80%) and $\mathcal{D}_{test}$ (20-30%).
4. **Model Selection:** Choose a classification algorithm suited to the data distribution and problem complexity.
5. **Training Phase:** The algorithm optimizes a loss function $J(\theta)$ over $\mathcal{D}_{train}$ to learn parameters $\theta$.
6. **Prediction Phase:** The trained model $h_\theta(\mathbf{x})$ predicts labels for unseen data in $\mathcal{D}_{test}$.
7. **Evaluation Phase:** Measure performance using metrics like accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Questions on "Explain the steps in building a classification model" or "Differentiate between training and test sets" are recurring 7-mark questions. Always mention the 70-30 split ratio and the purpose of cross-validation.

## 2.2 The Confusion Matrix: The Evaluator's Compass

The **Confusion Matrix** is an $N \times N$ table (where $N$ is the number of classes) that visualizes the performance of a classification model by comparing predicted labels against actual labels.

For **Binary Classification**, the matrix is $2 \times 2$:

| | **Predicted Positive** | **Predicted Negative** |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive** | True Positive (TP) | False Negative (FN) |
| **Actual Negative** | False Positive (FP) | True Negative (TN) |

### 2.2.1 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Metrics)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA SHEET — Memorize These for KTU ESE**

| Metric | Formula | Meaning |
|--------|---------|---------|
| **Accuracy** | $\text{Acc} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Overall correctness |
| **Error Rate** | $\text{Err} = 1 - \text{Acc} = \frac{FP + FN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Overall misclassification rate |
| **Precision (Positive Predictive Value)** | $P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Of predicted positives, how many are correct |
| **Recall (Sensitivity / True Positive Rate)** | $R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Of actual positives, how many were caught |
| **Specificity (True Negative Rate)** | $\text{Spec} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | Of actual negatives, how many were caught |
| **F1-Score (Harmonic Mean)** | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Balanced precision-recall metric |
| **False Positive Rate (FPR)** | $\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Used in ROC curves |
| **Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC)** | $\text{MCC} = \frac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}$ | Balanced metric for imbalanced data |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Pipe Symbol Rule:** In KTU exam answer sheets, never write $\vert x \vert$ as `|x|` inside markdown tables. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent rendering errors. This is a common typesetting mistake.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Classification algorithms are deployed in critical engineering and business applications:

- **Medical Diagnosis:** Classifying tumors as malignant or benign from MRI features.
- **Credit Card Fraud Detection:** Flagging transactions as fraudulent or legitimate in real-time.
- **Spam Filtering:** Classifying emails as spam or ham using text features.
- **Image Recognition:** Classifying objects in autonomous vehicles (pedestrian, car, road sign).
- **Customer Churn Prediction:** Classifying telecom subscribers as likely-to-churn or retained.
- **Sentiment Analysis:** Classifying product reviews as positive, negative, or neutral.

## 2.4 The Bias-Variance Tradeoff (Foundational Concept)

A classifier's generalization error can be decomposed into three components:

$$E[(\hat{y} - y)^2] = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \text{Irreducible Error}$$

- **Bias:** Error from wrong assumptions in the learning algorithm. High bias → **Underfitting**.
- **Variance:** Error from sensitivity to small fluctuations in the training set. High variance → **Overfitting**.
- **Irreducible Error:** Noise inherent in the data that no model can eliminate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** A high-bias model is like a student who oversimplifies and ignores important details. A high-variance model is like a student who memorizes the textbook word-for-word but fails on new questions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Mathematical Formulation of Classification

### 3.1.1 The Decision Function

A classifier learns a decision function $h_\theta: \mathbb{R}^d \rightarrow \{1, 2, \ldots, k\}$ that maps a $d$-dimensional feature vector to one of $k$ class labels. The predicted class for a new instance $\mathbf{x}$ is:

$$\hat{y} = h_\theta(\mathbf{x}) = \arg\max_{c \in C} P(y = c \mid \mathbf{x}; \theta)$$

This is the **Maximum A Posteriori (MAP)** decision rule — the model predicts the class with the highest posterior probability.

### 3.1.2 Bayes' Theorem Derivation (Foundational)

The posterior probability $P(y \mid \mathbf{x})$ is computed using **Bayes' Theorem**:

$$P(y \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid y) \cdot P(y)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Starting from the definition of conditional probability:

$$P(y \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(y \cap \mathbf{x})}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

Since $P(y \cap \mathbf{x}) = P(\mathbf{x} \mid y) \cdot P(y)$ (chain rule of probability), we substitute:

$$P(y \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid y) \cdot P(y)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

**Component Interpretation:**
- $P(y)$ → **Prior Probability:** Belief about class $y$ before seeing evidence.
- $P(\mathbf{x} \mid y)$ → **Likelihood:** Probability of observing features $\mathbf{x}$ given class $y$.
- $P(\mathbf{x})$ → **Evidence:** Marginal probability of the features (normalizing constant).
- $P(y \mid \mathbf{x})$ → **Posterior Probability:** Updated belief about $y$ after observing $\mathbf{x}$.

## 3.2 Logistic Regression: Detailed Derivation

**Logistic Regression** is a linear classifier that models the probability of a binary outcome using the **sigmoid (logistic) function**.

### 3.2.1 The Sigmoid Function

The sigmoid function $\sigma(z)$ squashes any real-valued input $z$ to the range $(0, 1)$:

$$\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

**Step-by-Step Properties of the Sigmoid:**

1. **Range:** As $z \rightarrow +\infty$, $\sigma(z) \rightarrow 1$.
2. **Range:** As $z \rightarrow -\infty$, $\sigma(z) \rightarrow 0$.
3. **Symmetry:** $\sigma(-z) = 1 - \sigma(z)$.
4. **Derivative:** $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$.

**Derivation of the Sigmoid Derivative:**

$$\frac{d}{dz}\sigma(z) = \frac{d}{dz}\left(\frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}\right) = \frac{e^{-z}}{(1 + e^{-z})^2}$$

We factor the expression as follows:

$$\frac{e^{-z}}{(1 + e^{-z})^2} = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}} \cdot \frac{e^{-z}}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

Substituting $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ and $1 - \sigma(z) = \frac{e^{-z}}{1 + e^{-z}}$:

$$\frac{d}{dz}\sigma(z) = \sigma(z) \cdot (1 - \sigma(z))$$

### 3.2.2 The Logistic Regression Hypothesis

The linear combination of features is computed as:

$$z = \theta^T \mathbf{x} = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x_1 + \theta_2 x_2 + \ldots + \theta_d x_d$$

Applying the sigmoid:

$$h_\theta(\mathbf{x}) = \sigma(\theta^T \mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\theta^T \mathbf{x}}}$$

This represents $P(y = 1 \mid \mathbf{x}; \theta)$. The decision rule is:

$$\hat{y} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } h_\theta(\mathbf{x}) \geq 0.5 \\ 0 & \text{if } h_\theta(\mathbf{x}) < 0.5 \end{cases}$$

### 3.2.3 The Cost Function (Log-Loss)

The cost function for a single training example is the **Binary Cross-Entropy (Log-Loss)**:

$$\text{Cost}(h_\theta(\mathbf{x}), y) = \begin{cases} -\log(h_\theta(\mathbf{x})) & \text{if } y = 1 \\ -\log(1 - h_\theta(\mathbf{x})) & \text{if } y = 0 \end{cases}$$

This can be compactly written as:

$$\text{Cost}(h_\theta(\mathbf{x}), y) = -y \log(h_\theta(\mathbf{x})) - (1-y)\log(1 - h_\theta(\mathbf{x}))$$

**The total cost function over $m$ training examples is:**

$$J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log(h_\theta(\mathbf{x}^{(i)})) + (1-y^{(i)})\log(1 - h_\theta(\mathbf{x}^{(i)})) \right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Log-Loss?** The squared error loss is non-convex when used with the sigmoid function, making optimization difficult. The log-loss is convex, ensuring gradient descent converges to the global minimum.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Confusion Matrix Computation

**Problem Statement (KTU Exam Style):**
A binary classifier was tested on 200 instances. The results are:
- True Positives (TP) = 50
- False Positives (FP) = 10
- False Negatives (FN) = 20
- True Negatives (TN) = 120

**Compute: (a) Accuracy, (b) Precision, (c) Recall, (d) F1-Score.**

**Step 1: Verify the Confusion Matrix Totals**

$$TP + FP + FN + TN = 50 + 10 + 20 + 120 = 200 \checkmark$$

This matches the total number of instances, confirming the data is consistent.

**Step 2: Compute Accuracy**

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{50 + 120}{200} = \frac{170}{200} = 0.85$$

**Step 3: Compute Precision**

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{50}{50 + 10} = \frac{50}{60} = 0.833$$

**Step 4: Compute Recall**

$$\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{50}{50 + 20} = \frac{50}{70} = 0.714$$

**Step 5: Compute F1-Score**

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.833 \times 0.714}{0.833 + 0.714}$$

Computing the numerator:

$$2 \times 0.833 \times 0.714 = 2 \times 0.595 = 1.190$$

Computing the denominator:

$$0.833 + 0.714 = 1.547$$

Therefore:

$$F_1 = \frac{1.190}{1.547} = 0.769$$

**Final Answer:** Accuracy = 85%, Precision = 83.3%, Recall = 71.4%, F1-Score = 76.9%
